AI.result: Achieves 95.65% average performance retention on standard VLM benchmarks while retaining only 30% of visual tokens, outperforming stage-wise baselines (94.3% retention). Shows improved robustness under same low-bit regime.
AI.conclusion: QUOTA provides an effective unified framework for efficient VLM deployment through collaborative quantization-and-pruning, addressing the brittleness of stage-wise approaches and enabling aggressive low-bit inference with minimal performance degradation.

title: R-FLoRA: Residual-Statistic-Gated Low-Rank Adaptation for Single-Image Face Morphing Attack Detection
comment: Pre-Print; Accepted in IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security (TIFS), 2026
summary: Face morphing attacks pose a substantial risk to the reliability of face recognition systems used in passport issuance, border control, and digital identity verification. Detecting morphing attacks from a single facial image remains challenging owing to the lack of a trusted reference and the diversity of attack generation methods. This paper presents a new Single-Image Face Morphing Attack Detection (S-MAD) framework that integrates high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with representations from a frozen, foundation-scale vision transformer. The approach employs residual-statistic-gated low-rank adapters (R-FLoRA) and feature-wise residual fusion (Res-FiLM) to enhance sensitivity to local morphing artefacts while preserving the semantic context of the backbone. A novel residual-contrastive alignment loss further regularises the fused token space, improving discrimination under unseen morphing conditions. Comprehensive experiments on four ICAO-compliant datasets, encompassing seven morph generation techniques, demonstrate that the proposed method consistently surpasses nine recent state-of-the-art S-MAD algorithms in detection accuracy and cross-domain (or dataset) generalisation. With a frozen backbone and minimal trainable parameters, the model achieves real-time efficiency and interpretability, making it suitable for real-life scenarios in biometric verification systems.

AI.tldr: A novel single-image face morphing attack detection framework that combines Laplacian residual statistics with frozen vision transformer features using gated adapters and fusion techniques, achieving state-of-the-art performance with real-time efficiency.
AI.motivation: Face morphing attacks threaten face recognition systems in critical applications like passport issuance and border control. Single-image detection is challenging due to lack of reference images and diverse attack generation methods.
AI.method: Integrates high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with frozen foundation-scale vision transformer representations. Uses residual-statistic-gated low-rank adapters (R-FLoRA) and feature-wise residual fusion (Res-FiLM) to detect local morphing artifacts while preserving semantic context. Includes residual-contrastive alignment loss for regularization.
AI.result: Outperforms nine recent state-of-the-art S-MAD algorithms across four ICAO-compliant datasets with seven morph generation techniques. Achieves superior detection accuracy and cross-domain generalization with frozen backbone and minimal trainable parameters.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides real-time, interpretable, and efficient morphing attack detection suitable for real-world biometric verification systems, addressing critical security vulnerabilities in face recognition applications.

title: Robust Diabetic Retinopathy Grading Using Dual-Resolution Attention-Based Deep Learning with Ordinal Regression
summary: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of vision impairment worldwide, and automated grading systems play a crucial role in large-scale screening programs. However, deep learning models often exhibit degraded performance when deployed across datasets acquired under different imaging conditions. This study presents a robust dual-resolution deep learning framework for DR grading that integrates attention-based feature fusion with ordinal regression to improve cross-dataset generalization. The proposed method employs two parallel EfficientNet backbones operating at different spatial resolutions to capture complementary retinal features. A learnable attention mechanism adaptively fuses multi-resolution representations, while an ordinal regression formulation based on the cumulative link model (CORAL) explicitly accounts for the ordered nature of DR severity levels. To mitigate domain discrepancies between datasets, a preprocessing strategy combining circular cropping, contrast enhancement, and histogram matching is applied. The model was trained on the APTOS 2019 dataset and evaluated on both an internal validation split and an external Messidor-2 test set. Experimental results demonstrate strong grading performance, achieving a quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.88 on the APTOS validation set and 0.68 on the unseen Messidor-2 dataset, indicating improved robustness for cross-dataset DR grading applications.

AI.tldr: A dual-resolution deep learning framework with attention-based feature fusion and ordinal regression improves cross-dataset generalization for diabetic retinopathy grading.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models for diabetic retinopathy grading often degrade when deployed across datasets with different imaging conditions, limiting their practical utility in large-scale screening programs.
AI.method: Uses two parallel EfficientNet backbones at different resolutions, learnable attention mechanism for feature fusion, ordinal regression (CORAL) for severity ordering, and preprocessing (circular cropping, contrast enhancement, histogram matching) for domain adaptation.
AI.result: Achieved QWK of 0.88 on APTOS validation set and 0.68 on unseen Messidor-2 dataset, demonstrating improved cross-dataset robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-resolution framework with attention fusion and ordinal regression enhances generalization across datasets, making it more suitable for real-world DR screening applications.

title: When Text Hijacks Vision: Benchmarking and Mitigating Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination in Vision Language Models
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have substantially enhanced their ability across multimodal video understanding benchmarks spanning temporal, action, object, and spatial understanding. However, we identify a critical yet overlooked issue: when embedded on-screen text contradicts the visual scene, existing VLMs systematically hallucinate, prioritizing overlay textual semantics over the actual visual content. We define this phenomenon as Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH). In this work, we propose VisualTextTrap, the first comprehensive benchmark, including large-scale human-validated samples with specifically designed evaluation metrics. In particular, we construct VisualTextTrap from widely-used public datasets using a scalable hybrid pipeline of VLMs assisted text generation and rigorous manual verification. The benchmark features 6,057 samples annotated across 88 fine-grained attributes within four dimensions, with hallucination intensity quantified on a five-level scale (L1--L5) that reflects the semantic contradiction between overlay text and visual reality. Moreover, we propose Visual Text Hallucination Mitigation Mixture-of-Experts (VTHM-MoE), a novel Vision-Text Disentanglement framework that employs a dual-encoder architecture. Concretely, four dimension-specialized expert modules spanning Temporal, Action, Object, and Spatial reasoning are first pre-trained to identify and leverage cross-modal discrepancies between textual semantics and actual video content. We develop an Adaptive Token Routing Strategy to enable dynamic expert allocation, conferring robust resistance to TOIH while preserving performance on uncontaminated videos. Extensive experiments conducted on our VisualTextTrap benchmark verify the effectiveness of VTHM-MoE, outperforming state-of-the-art counterparts with diverse video question answering tasks.

AI.tldr: VLMs hallucinate when on-screen text contradicts visuals (TOIH). VisualTextTrap benchmark quantifies this with 6K samples. VTHM-MoE framework uses dual-encoder + MoE experts to mitigate hallucination.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs systematically hallucinate when on-screen text contradicts visual scenes, prioritizing overlay text over actual visual content. This Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH) is a critical but overlooked issue in multimodal video understanding.
AI.method: 1) Created VisualTextTrap benchmark with 6,057 human-validated samples across 88 attributes in 4 dimensions (Temporal, Action, Object, Spatial), quantified on 5-level hallucination scale. 2) Proposed VTHM-MoE framework with dual-encoder architecture and 4 dimension-specialized expert modules. 3) Developed Adaptive Token Routing Strategy for dynamic expert allocation.
AI.result: VTHM-MoE outperforms state-of-the-art counterparts on diverse video QA tasks, demonstrating robust resistance to TOIH while preserving performance on uncontaminated videos. The benchmark provides comprehensive evaluation of hallucination.
AI.conclusion: TOIH is a significant problem in VLMs that requires specialized mitigation. The proposed VisualTextTrap benchmark and VTHM-MoE framework effectively address this issue through disentangled vision-text processing and adaptive expert routing.

title: Towards Generalizable Deepfake Image Detection with Vision Transformers
summary: In today's day and age, we face a challenge in detecting deepfake images because of the fast evolution of modern generative models and the poor generalization capability of existing methods. In this paper, we use an ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers like DINOv2, AIMv2 and OpenCLIP's ViT-L/14 to create generalizable method to detect deepfakes. We use the DF-Wild dataset released as part of the IEEE SP Cup 2025, because it uses a challenging and diverse set of manipulations and generation techniques. We started our experiments with CNN classifiers trained on spatial features. Experimental results show that our ensemble outperforms individual models and strong CNN baselines, achieving an AUC of 96.77% and an Equal Error Rate (EER) of just 9% on the DF-Wild test set, beating the state-of-the-art deepfake detection algorithm Effort by 7.05% and 8% in AUC and EER respectively. This was the winning solution for SP Cup, presented at ICASSP 2025.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a deepfake detection method using an ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers (DINOv2, AIMv2, OpenCLIP ViT-L/14) that achieves state-of-the-art performance on the DF-Wild dataset, winning the IEEE SP Cup 2025 competition.
AI.motivation: The rapid evolution of generative models and poor generalization of existing deepfake detection methods create challenges in detecting manipulated images, necessitating more robust and generalizable approaches.
AI.method: Uses an ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers including DINOv2, AIMv2, and OpenCLIP's ViT-L/14, trained on the DF-Wild dataset which contains diverse manipulation techniques. Started with CNN classifiers on spatial features as baseline.
AI.result: The ensemble method achieves 96.77% AUC and 9% EER on DF-Wild test set, outperforming individual models and CNN baselines, and beating the state-of-the-art Effort algorithm by 7.05% in AUC and 8% in EER.
AI.conclusion: The vision transformer ensemble approach provides a highly effective and generalizable solution for deepfake detection, demonstrated by winning the IEEE SP Cup 2025 competition and outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods.

title: SpatialImaginer: Towards Adaptive Visual Imagination for Spatial Reasoning
summary: Spatial intelligence, which refers to the ability to reason about geometric and physical structure from visual observations, remains a core challenge for multimodal large language models. Despite promising performance, recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often exhibit fragile reasoning traces in spatial intelligence tasks that involve consistent spatial state recognition. We argue that these failures stem from a mismatch between the spatial recognition mechanism and the text-only reasoning behavior of these MLLMs. Effective spatial reasoning requires low-level geometric structure to be faithfully preserved and updated throughout the reasoning process, whereas textual representations tend to abstract away precisely these critical details. To address this issue, we propose SpatialImaginer, a unified multimodal generation framework that integrates textual reasoning with visual imagination. Our framework adopts a divide-and-conquer strategy, using text chain-of-thought for high-level semantic planning and the visual imagination for geometry-sensitive state transformation and consistency preservation. To support this capability, we further introduce a difficulty-aware data engine with closed-loop verification to train the model to invoke visual imagination selectively when stable spatial state tracking is required. Extensive experiments on diverse spatial intelligence benchmarks show that SpatialImaginer achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially improves robustness on complex multi-step spatial reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: SpatialImaginer is a multimodal framework that combines textual reasoning with visual imagination to improve spatial intelligence in MLLMs, achieving SOTA performance on spatial reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs have fragile reasoning in spatial intelligence tasks due to a mismatch between spatial recognition needs and text-only reasoning. Textual representations abstract away critical geometric details needed for consistent spatial state tracking.
AI.method: SpatialImaginer uses a divide-and-conquer strategy: text chain-of-thought for high-level semantic planning, and visual imagination for geometry-sensitive state transformation and consistency preservation. Includes difficulty-aware data engine with closed-loop verification to train selective visual imagination invocation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse spatial intelligence benchmarks and substantially improves robustness on complex multi-step spatial reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating textual reasoning with visual imagination addresses the fundamental mismatch in spatial intelligence tasks, enabling more robust and consistent spatial reasoning in MLLMs.

title: Deep learning based Non-Rigid Volume-to-Surface Registration for Brain Shift compensation Using Point Cloud
summary: Soft-tissue deformation remains a major limitation in image-guided neurosurgery, where intra-operative anatomy can deviate substantially from pre-operative imaging due to brain shift, compromising navigation accuracy and surgical safety. Existing compensation methods often rely on intra-operative MRI, CT, or ultrasound, which are disruptive and difficult to integrate repeatedly into the surgical workflow. In contrast, partial 3D cortical surfaces can be reconstructed as point clouds from stereoscopic microscopes or laser range scanners (LRS), capturing only a limited portion of the exposed cortex. This makes point cloud registration a practical alternative without interrupting surgery; however, such partial and noisy observations make deformation estimation highly challenging. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration, enabling dense displacement field estimation from sparse intra-operative surface observations without explicit point correspondences or volumetric intra-operative imaging. The network leverages multi-scale point-based feature extraction and a hierarchical deformation decoder to capture both global and local deformations. The key contribution lies in integrating partial intra-operative surface information into the full pre-operative point cloud domain, enabling implicit correspondence learning and dense deformation recovery under limited visibility. Quantitative results demonstrate accurate recovery of fine-scale deformations, achieving an Endpoint Error (EPE) of 1.13 +/- 0.75 mm and RMSE of 1.33 +/- 0.81 mm under challenging partial-surface conditions. The proposed approach supports automatic, workflow-compatible brain-shift compensation from sparse surface observations.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration that estimates dense brain shift from sparse intra-operative surface observations without requiring explicit point correspondences or volumetric intra-operative imaging.
AI.motivation: Brain shift during neurosurgery causes significant soft-tissue deformation that compromises navigation accuracy. Existing compensation methods using intra-operative MRI/CT/ultrasound are disruptive to surgical workflow, while partial cortical surface observations from microscopes or laser scanners are limited and noisy, making deformation estimation challenging.
AI.method: A deep learning-based framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration using multi-scale point-based feature extraction and hierarchical deformation decoder. The approach integrates partial intra-operative surface information into full pre-operative point cloud domain to enable implicit correspondence learning and dense deformation recovery under limited visibility.
AI.result: Achieved accurate recovery of fine-scale deformations with Endpoint Error (EPE) of 1.13 ± 0.75 mm and RMSE of 1.33 ± 0.81 mm under challenging partial-surface conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach enables automatic, workflow-compatible brain-shift compensation from sparse surface observations without disrupting surgery, addressing a major limitation in image-guided neurosurgery.

title: MESA: A Training-Free Multi-Exemplar Deep Framework for Restoring Ancient Inscription Textures
summary: Ancient inscriptions frequently suffer missing or corrupted regions from fragmentation, erosion, or other damage, hindering reading, and analysis. We review prior image restoration methods and their applicability to inscription image recovery, then introduce MESA (Multi-Exemplar, Style-Aware) -an image-level restoration method that uses well-preserved exemplar inscriptions (from the same epigraphic monument, material, or similar letterforms) to guide reconstruction of damaged text. MESA encodes VGG19 convolutional features as Gram matrices to capture exemplar texture, style, and stroke structure; for each neural network layer it selects the exemplar minimizing Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD) to the damaged input. Layer-wise contribution weights are derived from Optical Character Recognition-estimated character widths in the exemplar set to bias filters toward scales matching letter geometry, and a training mask preserves intact regions so synthesis is restricted to damaged areas. We also summarize prior network architectures and exemplar and single-image synthesis, inpainting, and Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) approaches, highlighting limitations that MESA addresses. Comparative experiments demonstrate the advantages of MESA. Finally, we provide a practical roadmap for choosing restoration strategies given available exemplars and metadata.

AI.tldr: MESA is an image restoration method for damaged inscriptions that uses well-preserved exemplar inscriptions to guide reconstruction, employing VGG19 features, Gram matrices, and OCR-based weighting to preserve style and structure.
AI.motivation: Ancient inscriptions often have missing or corrupted regions due to fragmentation and erosion, making them difficult to read and analyze. Existing restoration methods have limitations in handling inscription-specific challenges like style preservation and structural accuracy.
AI.method: MESA uses VGG19 convolutional features encoded as Gram matrices to capture exemplar texture, style, and stroke structure. It selects exemplars minimizing Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD) per layer, uses OCR-estimated character widths to weight layer contributions, and employs a training mask to restrict synthesis to damaged areas only.
AI.result: Comparative experiments demonstrate MESA's advantages over prior methods, showing improved restoration quality for damaged inscriptions while preserving original style and structure.
AI.conclusion: MESA effectively addresses limitations of previous restoration approaches and provides a practical framework for choosing appropriate restoration strategies based on available exemplars and metadata.

title: Speculative Decoding for Autoregressive Video Generation
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion is emerging as a promising paradigm for streaming video synthesis, with step distillation serving as the primary means of accelerating inference. Whether speculative decoding, the dominant acceleration strategy for large language models, can be effectively adapted to autoregressive video generation remains an open question, because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors with no token-level distribution for exact rejection sampling. We introduce SDVG, which brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion by replacing token verification with an image-quality router. A 1.3B drafter proposes candidate blocks via four denoising steps; each block is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation--taking the minimum per-frame reward to catch single-frame artifacts that averaging would mask. Blocks scoring above a fixed threshold tau are accepted into the 14B target's KV cache; the rest are regenerated by the target. Two additional design choices prove critical: the first block is always force-rejected to anchor scene composition, and tau serves as a single knob that traces a smooth quality-speed Pareto frontier. On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at a 1.59x speedup with tau=-0.7, and reaches 2.09x at 95.7% quality retention--while consistently outperforming draft-only generation by over +17%. The framework is training-free, requires no architectural changes, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing autoregressive video generation pipelines.

AI.tldr: SDVG adapts speculative decoding to autoregressive video diffusion by using an image-quality router instead of token verification, achieving 1.59-2.09x speedup while maintaining 95.7-98.1% of target model quality.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding has been highly effective for accelerating large language models, but its adaptation to autoregressive video generation is challenging because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors without token-level distributions for exact rejection sampling.
AI.method: SDVG uses a 1.3B drafter model to propose candidate video blocks via four denoising steps. Each block is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation (minimum per-frame reward). Blocks scoring above threshold τ are accepted into the 14B target's KV cache; rejected blocks are regenerated by the target. Key design: first block always force-rejected to anchor scene composition.
AI.result: On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at 1.59x speedup with τ=-0.7, and reaches 2.09x speedup at 95.7% quality retention. Consistently outperforms draft-only generation by over +17%.
AI.conclusion: SDVG successfully adapts speculative decoding to autoregressive video diffusion, providing a training-free framework with no architectural changes that can be seamlessly integrated into existing pipelines, offering a smooth quality-speed Pareto frontier controlled by a single threshold parameter.

title: Where to Focus: Query-Modulated Multimodal Keyframe Selection for Long Video Understanding
summary: Long video understanding remains a formidable challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) due to the prohibitive computational cost of processing dense frame sequences. Prevailing solutions, which select a keyframe subset, typically rely on either a single visual-centric metric (e.g., CLIP similarity) or a static fusion of heuristic scores. This ``one-size-fits-all'' paradigm frequently fails: visual-only metrics are ineffective for plot-driven narrative queries, while indiscriminately incorporating textual scores introduces severe ``modal noise'' for purely visual tasks. To break this bottleneck, we propose Q-Gate, a plug-and-play and training-free framework that treats keyframe selection as a dynamic modality routing problem. We decouple the retrieval process into three lightweight expert streams: Visual Grounding for local details, Global Matching for scene semantics, and Contextual Alignment for subtitle-driven narratives. Crucially, Q-Gate introduces a Query-Modulated Gating Mechanism that leverages the in-context reasoning of an LLM to assess the query's intent and dynamically allocate attention weights across the experts. This mechanism intelligently activates necessary modalities while ``muting'' irrelevant ones, thereby maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio. Extensive experiments on LongVideoBench and Video-MME across multiple MLLM backbones demonstrate that Q-Gate substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. By effectively suppressing modality-specific noise, it provides a robust, highly interpretable solution for scalable video reasoning.

AI.tldr: Q-Gate is a training-free framework that dynamically routes keyframe selection through three expert streams based on query intent, outperforming SOTA methods on long video understanding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current keyframe selection methods for long video understanding in MLLMs use either visual-only metrics (ineffective for narrative queries) or static fusion of heuristic scores (introduces modal noise for visual tasks). This one-size-fits-all approach fails to adapt to different query types.
AI.method: Q-Gate decouples retrieval into three lightweight expert streams: Visual Grounding (local details), Global Matching (scene semantics), and Contextual Alignment (subtitle-driven narratives). It uses a Query-Modulated Gating Mechanism where an LLM assesses query intent to dynamically allocate attention weights across experts, activating relevant modalities while muting irrelevant ones.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on LongVideoBench and Video-MME across multiple MLLM backbones show Q-Gate substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by effectively suppressing modality-specific noise.
AI.conclusion: Q-Gate provides a robust, highly interpretable solution for scalable video reasoning by treating keyframe selection as a dynamic modality routing problem, maximizing signal-to-noise ratio through intelligent modality activation.

title: Long-CODE: Isolating Pure Long-Context as an Orthogonal Dimension in Video Evaluation
summary: As video generation models achieve unprecedented capabilities, the demand for robust video evaluation metrics becomes increasingly critical. Traditional metrics are intrinsically tailored for short-video evaluation, predominantly assessing frame-level visual quality and localized temporal smoothness. However, as state-of-the-art video generation models scale to generate longer videos, these metrics fail to capture essential long-range characteristics, such as narrative richness and global causal consistency. Recognizing that short-term visual perception and long-context attributes are fundamentally orthogonal dimensions, we argue that long-video metrics should be disentangled from short-video assessments. In this paper, we focus on the rigorous justification and design of a dedicated framework for long-video evaluation. We first introduce a suite of long-video attribute corruption tests, exposing the critical limitations of existing hort-video metrics from their insensitivity to structural inconsistencies, such as shot-level perturbations and narrative shuffling. To bridge this gap, we design a novel long-video metric based on shot dynamics, which is highly sensitive to the long-range testing framework. Furthermore, we introduce Long-CODE (Long-Context as an Orthogonal Dimension for video Evaluation), a specialized dataset designed to benchmark long-video evaluation, with human annotations isolated specifically to genuine long-range characteristics. Extensive experiments show that our proposed metrics achieve state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments. Ultimately, our metric and benchmark seamlessly complement existing short-video standards, establishing a holistic and unbiased evaluation paradigm for video generation models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new evaluation framework for long videos, arguing that existing metrics designed for short videos fail to capture long-range characteristics like narrative consistency. It introduces a shot-based metric and a specialized benchmark dataset (Long-CODE) for long-video evaluation.
AI.motivation: As video generation models advance to produce longer videos, traditional metrics designed for short videos are inadequate. They focus on frame-level visual quality and short-term smoothness but fail to assess essential long-range attributes like narrative richness and global causal consistency.
AI.method: 1) Introduced long-video attribute corruption tests to expose limitations of existing metrics; 2) Designed a novel long-video metric based on shot dynamics sensitive to long-range characteristics; 3) Created Long-CODE dataset with human annotations specifically for genuine long-range video attributes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed metrics achieve state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments. The framework successfully identifies structural inconsistencies (shot-level perturbations, narrative shuffling) that traditional metrics miss.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a holistic evaluation paradigm where long-video metrics complement existing short-video standards. This disentangled approach provides unbiased assessment of video generation models across both short-term visual perception and long-context attributes.

title: DEM Refinement and Validation on the Lunar Surface Using Shape-from-Shading with Chandrayaan-2 OHRC Imagery
summary: This study presents a Shape from Shading (SfS) framework to enhance sub-metre resolution lunar digital elevation models (DEMs) using imagery from the Orbiter High Resolution Camera (OHRC) aboard Chandrayaan-2. The framework applies SfS to an independent OHRC image of the same region, enabling SfS not just as a refinement tool, but as a source of new topographic data, unconstrained by stereo baseline limitations. The method is applied across three lunar sites, including the Cyrillus crater, the Vikram landing region, and the lunar south pole (Mons Mouton), with a systematic three-stage parameter sweep on the SfS smoothness weight. Results show measurable topographic enhancement, particularly in surface slope statistics, revealing fine-scale crater morphology previously unresolved. A limiting case is also characterized, where large pitch angle separation between the shading image and stereo pair reduces SfS sensitivity, and partial footprint coverage of the shading image is identified as a factor influencing spatially variable enhancement quality.

AI.tldr: A Shape from Shading framework enhances lunar DEMs using Chandrayaan-2 OHRC imagery, providing new topographic data beyond stereo limitations, with measurable improvements in fine-scale crater morphology.
AI.motivation: To improve sub-metre resolution lunar digital elevation models (DEMs) by overcoming stereo baseline limitations and extracting additional topographic information from single images using Shape from Shading techniques.
AI.method: Shape from Shading framework applied to independent OHRC images from Chandrayaan-2, with systematic three-stage parameter sweep on SfS smoothness weight across three lunar sites (Cyrillus crater, Vikram landing region, lunar south pole).
AI.result: Measurable topographic enhancement, particularly in surface slope statistics, revealing fine-scale crater morphology previously unresolved. Identified limitations include reduced SfS sensitivity with large pitch angle separation and variable enhancement quality with partial footprint coverage.
AI.conclusion: SfS can effectively enhance lunar DEMs as both a refinement tool and source of new topographic data, though performance depends on imaging geometry and coverage conditions.

title: Attention Is not Everything: Efficient Alternatives for Vision
summary: Recently computer vision has seen advancements mainly thanks to Transformer-based models. However many non-Transformer methods are still doing well being a direct competition of Transformer-based models. This review tries to present a comprehensive taxonomy of such methods and organize these methods into categories like convolution-based models, MLP-based models, state-space-based and more. These methods are looked at in terms of how efficient they are, how well they scale, how easy they are to understand and how robust they are. A total of 40 papers were chosen for this study. The goal is to give a view of non-Transformer methods and find out what challenges and opportunities exist for future computer vision research.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive review and taxonomy of non-Transformer computer vision methods, categorizing them into convolution-based, MLP-based, state-space-based models and analyzing their efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness.
AI.motivation: Despite recent advancements in Transformer-based models dominating computer vision research, many non-Transformer methods remain competitive and deserve systematic examination to understand their strengths, limitations, and future potential.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic review of 40 selected papers, organizing non-Transformer methods into categories including convolution-based models, MLP-based models, and state-space-based approaches. They analyze these methods across multiple dimensions: computational efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness.
AI.result: The review provides a comprehensive taxonomy and comparative analysis of non-Transformer computer vision methods, highlighting their competitive performance against Transformer-based approaches and identifying key characteristics across different architectural paradigms.
AI.conclusion: Non-Transformer methods remain viable alternatives to Transformer-based models in computer vision, offering different trade-offs in efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness. The review identifies challenges and opportunities for future research in developing more efficient and interpretable vision architectures beyond the Transformer paradigm.

title: HyKey: Hyperspectral Keypoint Detection and Matching in Minimally Invasive Surgery
summary: Purpose: 3D reconstruction in minimally invasive surgery (MIS) enables enhanced surgical guidance through improved visualisation, tool tracking, and augmented reality. However, traditional RGB-based keypoint detection and matching pipelines struggle with surgical challenges, such as poor texture and complex illumination. We investigate whether using snapshot hyperspectral imaging (HSI) can provide improved results on keypoint detection and matching surgical scenes. Methods: We developed HyKey, a HYperspectral KEYpoint detection and description model made up of a hybrid 3D-2D convolutional neural network that jointly extracts spatial-spectral features from HSI. The model was trained using synthetic homographic augmentation and epipolar geometry constraints on a robotically-acquired dual-camera RGB-HSI laparoscopic dataset of ex-vivo organs with calibrated camera poses. We benchmarked performance against established RGB-based methods, including SuperPoint and ALIKE. Results: Our HSI-based model outperformed RGB baselines on registered RGB frames, achieving 96.62% mean matching accuracy and 67.18% mean average accuracy at 10 degree on pose estimation, demonstrating consistent improvements across multiple evaluation metrics. Conclusion: Integrating spectral information from an HSI cube offers a promising approach for robust monocular 3D reconstruction in MIS, addressing limitations of texture-poor surgical environments through enhanced spectral-spatial feature discrimination. Our model and dataset are available at https://github.com/alexsaikia/HyKey-Hyperspectral-Keypoint-Detection

AI.tldr: HyKey, a hyperspectral keypoint detection model, outperforms RGB methods in surgical 3D reconstruction by leveraging spectral-spatial features to overcome texture-poor environments.
AI.motivation: Traditional RGB-based keypoint detection struggles in minimally invasive surgery due to poor texture and complex illumination. The authors investigate whether hyperspectral imaging can provide better keypoint detection and matching for surgical 3D reconstruction.
AI.method: Developed HyKey, a hybrid 3D-2D CNN that extracts spatial-spectral features from hyperspectral images. Trained using synthetic homographic augmentation and epipolar geometry constraints on a dual-camera RGB-HSI laparoscopic dataset of ex-vivo organs with calibrated poses.
AI.result: HSI-based model outperformed RGB baselines (SuperPoint, ALIKE) on registered RGB frames, achieving 96.62% mean matching accuracy and 67.18% mean average accuracy at 10 degrees on pose estimation, with consistent improvements across multiple metrics.
AI.conclusion: Integrating spectral information from hyperspectral imaging offers a promising approach for robust monocular 3D reconstruction in minimally invasive surgery, addressing limitations of texture-poor surgical environments through enhanced spectral-spatial feature discrimination.

title: SegTTA: Training-Free Test-Time Augmentation for Zero-Shot Medical Imaging Segmentation
summary: Increasingly advanced data augmentation techniques have greatly aided clinical medical research, increasing data diversity and improving model generalization capabilities. Although most current basic models exhibit strong generalization abilities, image quality varies due to differences in equipment and operators. To address these challenges, we present SegTTA, a framework that improves medical image segmentation without model retraining by combining four augmentations (Gamma correction, Contrast enhancement, Gaussian blur, Gaussian noise) with weighted voting across multiple MedSAM2 checkpoints. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across three diverse datasets: healthy uterus segmentation, uterine myoma detection, and multi class hepatic structure segmentation. Ablation studies reveal that large organs benefit from intensity augmentations while small lesions require noise augmentations. The voting threshold controls the coverage precision trade off, enabling task specific optimization for different clinical requirements. Ultimately, on a multiclass hepatic vessel dataset, compared to MedSAM2 baselines, our method achieves an increase of 1.6 in mIoU and 1.9 in aIoU, along with a reduction of approximately 2.0 in HD95. Code will be available at https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/SegTTA.

AI.tldr: SegTTA improves medical image segmentation without retraining by combining four augmentations with weighted voting across MedSAM2 checkpoints, achieving better performance across diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: Medical image quality varies due to equipment and operator differences, creating challenges for segmentation models. While data augmentation helps, there's a need for methods that improve segmentation without model retraining to handle these quality variations.
AI.method: SegTTA combines four augmentations (Gamma correction, Contrast enhancement, Gaussian blur, Gaussian noise) with weighted voting across multiple MedSAM2 checkpoints. The framework uses test-time augmentation without requiring model retraining.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across three datasets: healthy uterus segmentation, uterine myoma detection, and multi-class hepatic structure segmentation. On hepatic vessel dataset, achieved +1.6 mIoU, +1.9 aIoU, and -2.0 HD95 reduction compared to MedSAM2 baselines.
AI.conclusion: SegTTA effectively improves medical image segmentation by adapting to image quality variations through test-time augmentation and weighted voting. Different augmentations benefit different organ sizes, and voting threshold allows task-specific optimization for clinical requirements.

title: HSG: Hyperbolic Scene Graph
summary: Scene graph representations enable structured visual understanding by modeling objects and their relationships, and have been widely used for multiview and 3D scene reasoning. Existing methods such as MSG learn scene graph embeddings in Euclidean space using contrastive learning and attention based association. However, Euclidean geometry does not explicitly capture hierarchical entailment relationships between places and objects, limiting the structural consistency of learned representations. To address this, we propose Hyperbolic Scene Graph (HSG), which learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space where hierarchical relationships are naturally encoded through geometric distance. Our results show that HSG improves hierarchical structure quality while maintaining strong retrieval performance. The largest gains are observed in graph level metrics: HSG achieves a PP IoU of 33.17 and the highest Graph IoU of 33.51, outperforming the best AoMSG variant (25.37) by 8.14, highlighting the effectiveness of hyperbolic representation learning for scene graph modeling. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/HSG.

AI.tldr: HSG learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space instead of Euclidean space to better capture hierarchical relationships, improving structural consistency while maintaining retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: Existing scene graph methods in Euclidean space fail to explicitly capture hierarchical entailment relationships between places and objects, limiting structural consistency of learned representations.
AI.method: Proposes Hyperbolic Scene Graph (HSG) which learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space where hierarchical relationships are naturally encoded through geometric distance.
AI.result: HSG improves hierarchical structure quality while maintaining strong retrieval performance, achieving PP IoU of 33.17 and Graph IoU of 33.51, outperforming best AoMSG variant by 8.14.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic representation learning is effective for scene graph modeling, better capturing hierarchical relationships through geometric properties of hyperbolic space.

title: From Adaptation to Generalization: Adaptive Visual Prompting for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Visual prompting has emerged as a powerful method for adapting pre-trained models to new domains without updating model parameters. However, existing prompting methods typically optimize a single prompt per domain and apply it uniformly to all inputs, limiting their ability to generalize under intra and inter-domain variability, which is especially critical in the medical field. To address this, we propose APEX, an Adaptive Prompt EXtraction framework that retrieves input-specific prompts from a learnable prompt memory. The memory stores diverse, domain-discriminative prompt representations and is queried via domain features extracted from the Fourier spectrum. To learn robust and discriminative domain features, we introduce a novel Low-Frequency Feature Contrastive (LFC) learning framework that clusters representations from the same domain while separating those from different domains. Extensive experiments on two medical segmentation tasks demonstrate that APEX significantly improves generalization across both seen and unseen domains. Furthermore, it complements any existing backbones and consistently enhances performance, confirming its effectiveness as a plug-and-play prompting solution in medical fields. The code is available at https://github.com/cetinkayaevren/apex/

AI.tldr: APEX is an adaptive prompting framework that retrieves input-specific prompts from a learnable memory using Fourier-based domain features, improving generalization in medical segmentation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing visual prompting methods use a single prompt per domain applied uniformly to all inputs, which limits generalization under intra- and inter-domain variability, especially critical in medical applications.
AI.method: APEX retrieves input-specific prompts from a learnable prompt memory using domain features extracted from Fourier spectrum. It uses Low-Frequency Feature Contrastive (LFC) learning to cluster same-domain representations and separate different-domain ones.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two medical segmentation tasks show APEX significantly improves generalization across both seen and unseen domains, works as a plug-and-play solution with any backbone, and consistently enhances performance.
AI.conclusion: APEX provides an effective adaptive prompting framework for medical vision tasks that addresses domain variability limitations of existing methods through input-specific prompt retrieval and robust domain feature learning.

title: UniMesh: Unifying 3D Mesh Understanding and Generation
summary: Recent advances in 3D vision have led to specialized models for either 3D understanding (e.g., shape classification, segmentation, reconstruction) or 3D generation (e.g., synthesis, completion, and editing). However, these tasks are often tackled in isolation, resulting in fragmented architectures and representations that hinder knowledge transfer and holistic scene modeling. To address these challenges, we propose UniMesh, a unified framework that jointly learns 3D generation and understanding within a single architecture. First, we introduce a novel Mesh Head that acts as a cross model interface, bridging diffusion based image generation with implicit shape decoders. Second, we develop Chain of Mesh (CoM), a geometric instantiation of iterative reasoning that enables user driven semantic mesh editing through a closed loop latent, prompting, and re generation cycle. Third, we incorporate a self reflection mechanism based on an Actor Evaluator Self reflection triad to diagnose and correct failures in high level tasks like 3D captioning. Experimental results demonstrate that UniMesh not only achieves competitive performance on standard benchmarks but also unlocks novel capabilities in iterative editing and mutual enhancement between generation and understanding. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/UniMesh. Website: https://aigeeksgroup.github.io/UniMesh.

AI.tldr: UniMesh is a unified framework that jointly learns 3D generation and understanding in a single architecture, featuring a Mesh Head interface, Chain of Mesh for iterative editing, and self-reflection mechanisms for error correction.
AI.motivation: Current 3D vision approaches are fragmented with specialized models for either understanding (classification, segmentation) or generation (synthesis, completion). This isolation hinders knowledge transfer and holistic scene modeling, creating a need for unified architectures.
AI.method: 1) Mesh Head: Cross-model interface bridging diffusion-based image generation with implicit shape decoders. 2) Chain of Mesh (CoM): Geometric instantiation of iterative reasoning enabling user-driven semantic mesh editing through closed-loop latent, prompting, and regeneration cycles. 3) Self-reflection mechanism: Actor-Evaluator-Self-reflection triad to diagnose and correct failures in high-level tasks like 3D captioning.
AI.result: UniMesh achieves competitive performance on standard benchmarks while unlocking novel capabilities in iterative editing and demonstrating mutual enhancement between generation and understanding tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified framework successfully bridges the gap between 3D generation and understanding, enabling holistic scene modeling and knowledge transfer through innovative architectural components and iterative reasoning mechanisms.

title: Dual-Anchoring: Addressing State Drift in Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation(VLN) requires an agent to navigate through 3D environments by following natural language instructions. While recent Video Large Language Models(Video-LLMs) have largely advanced VLN, they remain highly susceptible to State Drift in long scenarios. In these cases, the agent's internal state drifts away from the true task execution state, leading to aimless wandering and failure to execute essential maneuvers in the instruction. We attribute this failure to two distinct cognitive deficits: Progress Drift, where the agent fails to distinguish completed sub-goals from remaining ones, and Memory Drift, where the agent's history representations degrade, making it lose track of visited landmarks. In this paper, we propose a Dual-Anchoring Framework that explicitly anchors the instruction progress and history representations. First, to address progress drift, we introduce Instruction Progress Anchoring, which supervises the agent to generate structured text tokens that delineate completed versus remaining sub-goals. Second, to mitigate memory drift, we propose Memory Landmark Anchoring, which utilizes a Landmark-Centric World Model to retrospectively predict object-centric embeddings extracted by the Segment Anything Model, compelling the agent to explicitly verify past observations and preserve distinct representations of visited landmarks. Facilitating this framework, we curate two extensive datasets: 3.6 million samples with explicit progress descriptions, and 937k grounded landmark data for retrospective verification. Extensive experiments in both simulation and real-world environments demonstrate the superiority of our method, achieving a 15.2% improvement in Success Rate and a remarkable 24.7% gain on long-horizon trajectories. To facilitate further research, we will release our code, data generation pipelines, and the collected datasets.

AI.tldr: A Dual-Anchoring Framework addresses state drift in Vision-Language Navigation by anchoring instruction progress and history representations, improving performance on long-horizon tasks.
AI.motivation: Video-LLMs for VLN suffer from State Drift in long scenarios, causing agents to wander aimlessly and fail essential maneuvers due to two cognitive deficits: Progress Drift (confusion about completed vs remaining sub-goals) and Memory Drift (degradation of history representations causing loss of landmark tracking).
AI.method: Proposes a Dual-Anchoring Framework with two components: 1) Instruction Progress Anchoring - supervises agent to generate structured text tokens distinguishing completed vs remaining sub-goals; 2) Memory Landmark Anchoring - uses Landmark-Centric World Model to retrospectively predict object-centric embeddings from Segment Anything Model, forcing explicit verification of past observations and preservation of landmark representations. Created two datasets: 3.6M samples with progress descriptions and 937k grounded landmark data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world show superiority with 15.2% improvement in Success Rate and remarkable 24.7% gain on long-horizon trajectories.
AI.conclusion: The Dual-Anchoring Framework effectively mitigates state drift in VLN by explicitly anchoring progress and memory representations, significantly improving performance especially on challenging long-horizon navigation tasks. Code, data pipelines, and datasets will be released to facilitate further research.

title: Unveiling Deepfakes: A Frequency-Aware Triple Branch Network for Deepfake Detection
summary: Advanced deepfake technologies are blurring the lines between real and fake, presenting both revolutionary opportunities and alarming threats. While it unlocks novel applications in fields like entertainment and education, its malicious use has sparked urgent ethical and societal concerns ranging from identity theft to the dissemination of misinformation. To tackle these challenges, feature analysis using frequency features has emergedas a promising direction for deepfake detection. However, oneaspect that has been overlooked so far is that existing methodstend to concentrate on one or a few specific frequency domains,which risks overfitting to particular artifacts and significantlyundermines their robustness when facing diverse forgery patterns. Another underexplored aspect we observe is that different features often attend to the same forged region, resulting in redundant feature representations and limiting the diversity of the extracted clues. This may undermine the ability of a model to capture complementary information across different facets, thereby compromising its generalization capability to diverse manipulations. In this paper, we seek to tackle these challenges from two aspects: (1) we propose a triple-branch network that jointly captures spatial and frequency features by learning from both original image and image reconstructed by different frequency channels, and (2) we mathematically derive feature decoupling and fusion losses grounded in the mutual information theory, which enhances the model to focus on task-relevant features across the original image and the image reconstructed by different frequency channels. Extensive experiments on six large-scale benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance. Our code is released at https://github.com/injooker/Unveiling Deepfake.

AI.tldr: Proposes a triple-branch network with feature decoupling and fusion losses for robust deepfake detection by jointly learning spatial and frequency features while reducing redundancy.
AI.motivation: Deepfake technology poses serious threats like identity theft and misinformation. Existing detection methods overfit to specific frequency domains and suffer from feature redundancy, limiting robustness against diverse forgery patterns.
AI.method: Triple-branch network that jointly captures spatial and frequency features from original images and frequency-reconstructed images. Uses mathematically derived feature decoupling and fusion losses based on mutual information theory to focus on task-relevant features.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six large-scale benchmark datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance consistently across different evaluation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses limitations of existing deepfake detection methods by capturing complementary information across spatial and frequency domains while reducing feature redundancy, leading to improved robustness and generalization.

title: AutoVQA-G: Self-Improving Agentic Framework for Automated Visual Question Answering and Grounding Annotation
comment: Accepted at IEEE ICASSP 2026. 5 pages, 5 figures. Code available at https://github.com/rohnson1999/AutoVQA-G
summary: Manual annotation of high-quality visual question answering with grounding (VQA-G) datasets, which pair visual questions with evidential grounding, is crucial for advancing vision-language models (VLMs), but remains unscalable. Existing automated methods are often hindered by two key issues: (1) inconsistent data fidelity due to model hallucinations; (2) brittle verification mechanisms based on simple heuristics. To address these limitations, we introduce AutoVQA-G, a self-improving agentic framework for automated VQA-G annotation. AutoVQA-G employs an iterative refinement loop where a Consistency Evaluation module uses Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for fine-grained visual verification. Based on this feedback, a memory-augmented Prompt Optimization agent analyzes critiques from failed samples to progressively refine generation prompts. Our experiments show that AutoVQA-G generates VQA-G datasets with superior visual grounding accuracy compared to leading multimodal LLMs, offering a promising approach for creating high-fidelity data to facilitate more robust VLM training and evaluation. Code: https://github.com/rohnson1999/AutoVQA-G

AI.tldr: AutoVQA-G is a self-improving agentic framework that automatically generates high-quality VQA-G datasets with better visual grounding accuracy than existing methods, addressing model hallucinations and brittle verification issues.
AI.motivation: Manual annotation of VQA-G datasets is unscalable, while existing automated methods suffer from inconsistent data fidelity due to model hallucinations and brittle verification mechanisms based on simple heuristics.
AI.method: AutoVQA-G uses an iterative refinement loop with a Consistency Evaluation module employing Chain-of-Thought reasoning for fine-grained visual verification, and a memory-augmented Prompt Optimization agent that analyzes critiques from failed samples to progressively refine generation prompts.
AI.result: AutoVQA-G generates VQA-G datasets with superior visual grounding accuracy compared to leading multimodal LLMs, demonstrating better performance than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: AutoVQA-G offers a promising approach for creating high-fidelity VQA-G data to facilitate more robust vision-language model training and evaluation, addressing scalability and quality issues in dataset generation.

title: Coevolving Representations in Joint Image-Feature Diffusion
summary: Joint image-feature generative modeling has recently emerged as an effective strategy for improving diffusion training by coupling low-level VAE latents with high-level semantic features extracted from pre-trained visual encoders. However, existing approaches rely on a fixed representation space, constructed independently of the generative objective and kept unchanged during training. We argue that the representation space guiding diffusion should itself adapt to the generative task. To this end, we propose Coevolving Representation Diffusion (CoReDi), a framework in which the semantic representation space evolves during training by learning a lightweight linear projection jointly with the diffusion model. While naively optimizing this projection leads to degenerate solutions, we show that stable coevolution can be achieved through a combination of stop-gradient targets, normalization, and targeted regularization that prevents feature collapse. This formulation enables the semantic space to progressively specialize to the needs of image synthesis, improving its complementarity with image latents. We apply CoReDi to both VAE latent diffusion and pixel-space diffusion, demonstrating that adaptive semantic representations improve generative modeling across both settings. Experiments show that CoReDi achieves faster convergence and higher sample quality compared to joint diffusion models operating in fixed representation spaces.

AI.tldr: CoReDi introduces adaptive semantic representations that evolve during diffusion training, improving image synthesis by learning a lightweight projection jointly with the diffusion model.
AI.motivation: Existing joint image-feature generative models use fixed representation spaces constructed independently of the generative objective, which limits their effectiveness. The authors argue that representation spaces should adapt to the generative task rather than remain static.
AI.method: Proposes Coevolving Representation Diffusion (CoReDi) where semantic representation space evolves during training by learning a lightweight linear projection jointly with the diffusion model. Uses stop-gradient targets, normalization, and targeted regularization to prevent feature collapse and enable stable coevolution.
AI.result: CoReDi achieves faster convergence and higher sample quality compared to joint diffusion models using fixed representation spaces. Works effectively with both VAE latent diffusion and pixel-space diffusion.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive semantic representations that co-evolve with diffusion training improve generative modeling by allowing the representation space to specialize for image synthesis needs, enhancing complementarity with image latents.

title: Edit Fidelity Field: Semantics-Aware Region Isolation for Training-Free Scene Text Editing
summary: Scene text editing (STE) has achieved remarkable progress in accurately rendering target text through diffusion-based methods. However, we identify a critical yet overlooked problem: edit spillover -- when editing a target text region, existing methods inadvertently modify non-target regions, particularly neighboring text. Through systematic evaluation on 50 real-world scenes across four categories, we reveal that state-of-the-art diffusion editing models exhibit a spillover rate of 94%, meaning nearly all non-target text regions are altered during editing. To address this, we propose the Edit Fidelity Field (EFF), a semantics-aware continuous field that controls per-pixel editing fidelity. Unlike binary masks, EFF leverages OCR-detected text regions to construct a four-zone field: Edit Core (fully editable), Transition Zone (smooth decay), Protected Zone (non-target text, explicitly locked), and Background (strictly preserved). EFF operates as a training-free, model-agnostic post-processing module applicable to any diffusion-based STE method. We further propose per-region spillover quantification, a novel evaluation protocol that measures edit leakage at each non-target text region individually. Experiments demonstrate that EFF reduces spillover rate from 94% to 25% while improving non-target region preservation by +91.4 dB PSNR.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses "edit spillover" in scene text editing where editing target text inadvertently modifies neighboring non-target text. They propose Edit Fidelity Field (EFF) to control per-pixel editing fidelity and reduce spillover from 94% to 25%.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based scene text editing methods suffer from "edit spillover" - when editing target text, they inadvertently modify non-target regions, especially neighboring text. Systematic evaluation shows 94% spillover rate, meaning nearly all non-target text gets altered during editing.
AI.method: Proposes Edit Fidelity Field (EFF), a semantics-aware continuous field that controls per-pixel editing fidelity. Unlike binary masks, EFF uses OCR-detected text regions to create four zones: Edit Core (fully editable), Transition Zone (smooth decay), Protected Zone (non-target text, explicitly locked), and Background (strictly preserved). EFF is training-free, model-agnostic, and works as a post-processing module for any diffusion-based STE method.
AI.result: EFF reduces spillover rate from 94% to 25% while improving non-target region preservation by +91.4 dB PSNR. The method also introduces per-region spillover quantification as a novel evaluation protocol to measure edit leakage at each non-target text region individually.
AI.conclusion: The Edit Fidelity Field effectively addresses the critical edit spillover problem in scene text editing by providing precise control over editing fidelity, significantly reducing unintended modifications to non-target regions while maintaining editing quality.

title: RS-HyRe-R1: A Hybrid Reward Mechanism to Overcome Perceptual Inertia for Remote Sensing Images Understanding
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training substantially improves remote sensing vision-language models (RS-VLMs). However, when handling complex remote sensing imagery (RSI) requiring exhaustive visual scanning, models tend to rely on localized salient cues for rapid inference. We term this RL-induced bias "perceptual inertia". Driven by reward maximization, models favor quick outcome fitting, leading to two limitations: cognitively, overreliance on specific features impedes complete evidence construction; operationally, models struggle to flexibly shift visual focus across tasks. To address this bias and encourage comprehensive visual evidence mining, we propose RS-HyRe-R1, a hybrid reward framework for RSI understanding. It introduces: (1) a spatial reasoning activation reward that enforces structured visual reasoning; (2) a perception correctness reward that provides adaptive quality anchors across RS tasks, ensuring accurate geometric and semantic alignment; and (3) a visual-semantic path evolution reward that penalizes repetitive reasoning and promotes exploration of complementary cues to build richer evidence chains. Experiments show RS-HyRe-R1 effectively mitigates "perceptual inertia", encouraging deeper, more diverse reasoning. With only 3B parameters, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on REC, OVD, and VQA tasks, outperforming models up to 7B parameters. It also demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization, surpassing the second-best model by 3.16%, 3.97%, and 2.72% on VQA, OVD, and REC, respectively. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/geox-lab/RS-HyRe-R1.

AI.tldr: RS-HyRe-R1 is a hybrid reward framework that addresses "perceptual inertia" in RL-trained remote sensing vision-language models by promoting comprehensive visual evidence mining through spatial reasoning, perception correctness, and visual-semantic path evolution rewards.
AI.motivation: RL post-training causes remote sensing vision-language models to develop "perceptual inertia" - overreliance on localized salient cues for rapid inference, which impedes complete evidence construction and flexible visual focus shifting across complex remote sensing imagery tasks.
AI.method: Proposes RS-HyRe-R1 hybrid reward framework with three components: (1) spatial reasoning activation reward enforcing structured visual reasoning, (2) perception correctness reward providing adaptive quality anchors for geometric/semantic alignment, and (3) visual-semantic path evolution reward penalizing repetitive reasoning and promoting exploration of complementary cues.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on REC, OVD, and VQA tasks with only 3B parameters, outperforming models up to 7B parameters. Shows strong zero-shot generalization, surpassing second-best model by 3.16% (VQA), 3.97% (OVD), and 2.72% (REC).
AI.conclusion: RS-HyRe-R1 effectively mitigates RL-induced "perceptual inertia" in remote sensing vision-language models, encouraging deeper and more diverse reasoning while maintaining computational efficiency with smaller parameter counts.

title: Dual Strategies for Test-Time Adaptation
comment: Findings of Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: Conventional test-time adaptation (TTA) approaches typically adapt the model using only a small fraction of test samples, often those with low-entropy predictions, thereby failing to fully leverage the available information in the test distribution. This paper introduces DualTTA, a novel framework that improves performance under distribution shifts by utilizing a larger and more diverse set of test samples. DualTTA identifies two distinct groups: one where the model's predictions are likely consistent with the underlying semantics, and another where predictions are likely incorrect. For the first group, it minimizes prediction entropy to reinforce reliable decisions; for the second, it maximizes entropy to suppress overconfident errors and unlearn spurious behavior. These groups are adaptively selected using a new reliability criterion that measures prediction stability under both semantic-preserving and semantic-altering transformations, addressing the limitations of purely entropy-based selection. We further provide theoretical analysis and empirical justification showing that our approach enables a tighter separation between reliable and unreliable samples, in the context of their suitability for adaptation, leading to provably more effective model updates.

AI.tldr: DualTTA is a test-time adaptation framework that uses a dual strategy: minimizing entropy for reliable samples and maximizing entropy for unreliable samples, selected via a new reliability criterion based on prediction stability under transformations.
AI.motivation: Conventional TTA methods only use a small fraction of test samples (typically low-entropy ones), failing to fully leverage available test distribution information. This limits adaptation effectiveness under distribution shifts.
AI.method: DualTTA identifies two groups: reliable samples (consistent predictions) and unreliable samples (incorrect predictions). For reliable samples, it minimizes prediction entropy to reinforce decisions; for unreliable samples, it maximizes entropy to suppress errors. Samples are selected using a new reliability criterion measuring prediction stability under both semantic-preserving and semantic-altering transformations.
AI.result: The approach enables tighter separation between reliable and unreliable samples for adaptation, leading to provably more effective model updates. It provides theoretical analysis and empirical justification for improved performance under distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: DualTTA improves TTA performance by utilizing more diverse test samples through a dual entropy strategy with transformation-based reliability selection, addressing limitations of purely entropy-based methods.

title: UniGeo: Unifying Geometric Guidance for Camera-Controllable Image Editing via Video Models
summary:
Camera-controllable image editing aims to synthesize novel views of a given scene under varying camera poses while strictly preserving cross-view geometric consistency. However, existing methods typically rely on fragmented geometric guidance, such as only injecting point clouds at the representation level despite models containing multiple levels, and are mainly based on image diffusion models that operate on discrete view mappings. These two limitations jointly lead to geometric drift and structural degradation under continuous camera motion.
  We observe that while leveraging video models provides continuous viewpoint priors for camera-controllable image editing, they still struggle to form stable geometric understanding if geometric guidance remains fragmented. To systematically address this, we inject unified geometric guidance across three levels that jointly determine the generative output: representation, architecture, and loss function.
  To this end, we propose UniGeo, a novel camera-controllable editing framework. Specifically, at the representation level, UniGeo incorporates a frame-decoupled geometric reference injection mechanism to provide robust cross-view geometry context. At the architecture level, it introduces geometric anchor attention to align multi-view features. At the loss function level, it proposes a trajectory-endpoint geometric supervision strategy to explicitly reinforce the structural fidelity of target views.
  Comprehensive experiments across multiple public benchmarks, encompassing both extensive and limited camera motion settings, demonstrate that UniGeo significantly outperforms existing methods in both visual quality and geometric consistency.

AI.tldr: UniGeo: A unified geometric guidance framework for camera-controllable image editing that injects geometric consistency across representation, architecture, and loss function levels to prevent geometric drift and structural degradation under continuous camera motion.
AI.motivation: Existing camera-controllable image editing methods suffer from geometric drift and structural degradation due to fragmented geometric guidance (only at representation level) and reliance on image diffusion models with discrete view mappings. Video models provide continuous viewpoint priors but still lack stable geometric understanding when guidance is fragmented.
AI.method: UniGeo injects unified geometric guidance across three levels: 1) Representation level: frame-decoupled geometric reference injection for robust cross-view geometry context; 2) Architecture level: geometric anchor attention to align multi-view features; 3) Loss function level: trajectory-endpoint geometric supervision to explicitly reinforce structural fidelity of target views.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments across multiple public benchmarks show UniGeo significantly outperforms existing methods in both visual quality and geometric consistency, in both extensive and limited camera motion settings.
AI.conclusion: Systematically injecting geometric guidance across representation, architecture, and loss function levels is crucial for achieving stable geometric understanding and preventing drift in camera-controllable image editing, with UniGeo demonstrating superior performance over existing approaches.

title: Multi-Camera Self-Calibration in Sports Motion Capture: Leveraging Human and Stick Poses
summary: Multi-camera systems are widely employed in sports to capture the 3D motion of athletes and equipment, yet calibrating their extrinsic parameters remains costly and labor-intensive. We introduce an efficient, tool-free method for multi-camera extrinsic calibration tailored to sports involving stick-like implements (e.g., golf clubs, bats, hockey sticks). Our approach jointly exploits two complementary cues from synchronized multi-camera videos: (i) human body keypoints with unknown metric scale and (ii) a rigid stick-like implement of known length. We formulate a three-stage optimization pipeline that refines camera extrinsics, reconstructs human and stick trajectories, and resolves global scale via the stick-length constraint. Our method achieves accurate extrinsic calibration without dedicated calibration tools. To benchmark this task, we present the first dataset for multi-camera self-calibration in stick-based sports, consisting of synthetic sequences across four sports categories with 3 to 10 cameras. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that our method delivers SOTA performance, achieving low rotation and translation errors. Our project page: https://fandulu.github.io/sport_stick_multi_cam_calib/.

AI.tldr: Tool-free multi-camera calibration method for sports using human keypoints and stick-like implements of known length.
AI.motivation: Current multi-camera calibration for sports is costly and labor-intensive, requiring dedicated calibration tools. There's a need for efficient, tool-free calibration methods.
AI.method: Three-stage optimization pipeline using synchronized multi-camera videos: 1) human body keypoints (unknown metric scale), 2) rigid stick-like implement of known length. Jointly exploits both cues to refine camera extrinsics, reconstruct trajectories, and resolve global scale via stick-length constraint.
AI.result: Achieves accurate extrinsic calibration without dedicated tools. Creates first dataset for multi-camera self-calibration in stick-based sports (synthetic sequences across 4 sports with 3-10 cameras). Demonstrates SOTA performance with low rotation and translation errors.
AI.conclusion: Proposes an efficient, tool-free method for multi-camera calibration in stick-based sports that outperforms existing approaches and provides a benchmark dataset for future research.

title: PBSBench: A Multi-Level Vision-Language Framework and Benchmark for Hematopathology Whole Slide Image Interpretation
comment: 19 pages, 12 figures, Accepted by CVPR Findings 2026
summary: Peripheral Blood Smear (PBS) is a critical microscopic examination in hematopathology that yields whole-slide imaging (WSI). Unlike solid tissue pathology, PBS interpretation focuses on individual cell morphologies rather than tissue architecture, making it distinct in both visual characteristics and diagnostic reasoning. However, current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for pathology are primarily developed on solid-tissue WSIs and struggle to generalize to PBS. To bridge this gap, we construct PBSInstr, the first vision-language dataset for PBS interpretation, comprising 353 PBS WSIs paired with microscopic impression paragraphs and 29k cell-level image crops annotated with cell type labels and morphological descriptions. To facilitate instruction tuning, PBSInstr further includes 27k question-answer (QA) pairs for cell crops and 1,286 QA pairs for PBS slides. Building upon PBSInstr, we develop PBS-VL, a hematopathology-tailored vision-language model for multi-level PBS interpretation at both cell and slide levels. To comprehensively evaluate PBS understanding, we construct PBSBench, a visual question answering (VQA) benchmark featuring four question categories and six PBS interpretation tasks. Experiments show that PBS-VL outperforms existing general-purpose and pathology MLLMs, underscoring the value of PBS-specific data. We release our code, datasets, and model weights to facilitate future research. Our proposed framework lays the foundation for developing practical AI assistants supporting decision-making in hematopathology.

AI.tldr: PBS-VL: A vision-language model specialized for Peripheral Blood Smear interpretation, trained on PBSInstr dataset and evaluated on PBSBench benchmark, outperforming general pathology models.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LLMs for pathology are developed on solid-tissue WSIs and struggle with PBS interpretation, which focuses on individual cell morphologies rather than tissue architecture. There's a need for specialized models for hematopathology.
AI.method: Constructed PBSInstr dataset with 353 PBS WSIs, 29k cell crops with annotations, and 27k QA pairs. Developed PBS-VL model tailored for multi-level PBS interpretation. Created PBSBench benchmark with 4 question categories and 6 tasks for evaluation.
AI.result: PBS-VL outperforms existing general-purpose and pathology MLLMs, demonstrating the value of PBS-specific data. The model shows strong performance across various PBS interpretation tasks.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides foundation for AI assistants in hematopathology decision-making. PBS-specific data and models are crucial for accurate PBS interpretation. Released code, datasets, and model weights to facilitate research.

title: DGSSM: Diffusion guided state-space models for multimodal salient object detection
comment: Accepted at ICPR 2026. Diffusion-guided Mamba framework for multimodal salient object detection. Evaluated on 13 benchmarks (RGB, RGB-D, RGB-T)
summary: Salient object detection (SOD) requires modeling both long-range contextual dependencies and fine-grained structural details, which remains challenging for convolutional, transformer-based, and Mamba-based state space models. While recent Mamba-based state space approaches enable efficient global reasoning, they often struggle to recover precise object boundaries. In contrast, diffusion models capture strong structural priors through iterative denoising, but their use in discriminative dense prediction is still limited due to computational cost and integration challenges. In this work, we propose DGSSM, a diffusion-guided state space (Mamba) framework that formulates multimodal salient object detection as a progressive denoising process. The framework integrates diffusion structural priors with multi-scale state space encoding, adaptive saliency prompting, and an iterative Mamba diffusion refinement mechanism to improve boundary accuracy. A boundary-aware refinement head and self-distillation strategy further enhance spatial coherence and feature consistency. Extensive experiments on 13 public benchmarks across RGB, RGB-D, and RGB-T settings demonstrate that DGSSM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple evaluation metrics while maintaining a compact model size.
