AI.tldr: Vid-LLMs exhibit spatiotemporal sycophancy - retracting correct visual judgments to conform to misleading user feedback, fabricating unsupported explanations to justify incorrect revisions.
AI.motivation: Video Large Language Models show strong performance but their robustness under conversational interaction is underexplored, particularly their tendency to retract correct visual judgments when faced with misleading user feedback.
AI.method: Proposed a negation-based gaslighting evaluation framework and created GasVideo-1000 benchmark to probe spatiotemporal sycophancy with clear visual grounding and temporal reasoning requirements. Evaluated various state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary Vid-LLMs.
AI.result: Vulnerability to negation-based gaslighting is pervasive and severe across all tested models, even those with strong baseline performance. Prompt-level grounding constraints only partially mitigate the behavior and don't prevent hallucinated justifications or belief reversal.
AI.conclusion: Current Vid-LLMs lack robust mechanisms for maintaining grounded spatiotemporal beliefs under adversarial conversational feedback, revealing a critical weakness in their conversational robustness.

title: Exploring Boundary-Aware Spatial-Frequency Fusion for Camouflaged Object Detection
summary: Camouflaged Object Detection is challenging due to the high degree of similarity between camouflaged objects and their surrounding backgrounds. Current COD methods mainly rely on edge extraction in the spatial domain and local pixel-level information, neglecting the importance of global structural features. Additionally, they fail to effectively leverage the importance of phase spectrum information within frequency domain features. To this end, we propose a COD framework BASFNet based on boundary-aware frequency domain and spatial domain fusion.This method uses dual guided integration of frequency domain and spatial domain features. A phase-spectrum-based frequency-enhanced edge exploration module (FEEM) and a spatial core segmentation module (SCSM) are introduced to jointly capture the boundary and object features of camouflaged objects. These features are then effectively integrated through a spatial-frequency fusion interaction module (SFFIM). Furthermore, the boundary detection is further optimized through an boundary-aware training strategy. BASFNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on three benchmark datasets, validating the effectiveness of the fusion of frequency and spatial domain information in COD tasks.

AI.tldr: BASFNet is a camouflaged object detection framework that fuses frequency and spatial domain features using phase spectrum analysis and boundary-aware training to better detect objects that blend with their backgrounds.
AI.motivation: Current COD methods focus too much on edge extraction and local pixel information while neglecting global structural features and frequency domain information, especially phase spectrum features that could help distinguish camouflaged objects from backgrounds.
AI.method: Proposes BASFNet with dual guided integration of frequency and spatial domain features: 1) Frequency-Enhanced Edge Exploration Module (FEEM) using phase spectrum analysis, 2) Spatial Core Segmentation Module (SCSM) for object features, 3) Spatial-Frequency Fusion Interaction Module (SFFIM) for feature integration, and 4) boundary-aware training strategy.
AI.result: BASFNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on three benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of fusing frequency and spatial domain information for COD tasks.
AI.conclusion: The fusion of frequency domain (especially phase spectrum) and spatial domain features, combined with boundary-aware training, provides an effective solution for camouflaged object detection by better capturing both boundary and object features.

title: AeroRAG: Structured Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented LLM for Fine-Grained Aerial Visual Reasoning
summary: Despite recent progress in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), reliable visual question answering in aerial scenes remains challenging. In such scenes, task-critical evidence is often carried by small objects, explicit quantities, coarse locations, and inter-object relations, whereas conventional dense visual-token representations are not well aligned with these structured semantics. To address this interface mismatch, we propose AeroRAG, a scene-graph-guided multimodal retrieval-augmented generation framework for visual question answering. The framework first converts an input image into structured visual knowledge, including object categories, quantities, spatial locations, and semantic relations, and then retrieves query-relevant semantic chunks to construct compact prompts for a text-based large language model. Rather than relying on direct reasoning over dense visual tokens, our method introduces a more explicit intermediate interface between perception and language reasoning. Experiments on the AUG aerial dataset and the general-domain VG-150 benchmark show consistent improvements over six strong MLLM baselines, with the largest gains observed in dense aerial scenes and relation-sensitive reasoning. We further evaluate the framework on VQAv2 to verify that the proposed interface remains compatible with standard visual reasoning settings. These results suggest that structured retrieval is a practical design direction for deployment-oriented and grounded visual reasoning systems.

AI.tldr: AeroRAG is a scene-graph-guided multimodal retrieval-augmented generation framework that converts aerial images into structured visual knowledge (objects, quantities, locations, relations) and retrieves relevant semantic chunks to construct compact prompts for LLMs, improving visual question answering in aerial scenes.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with reliable visual question answering in aerial scenes where task-critical evidence involves small objects, explicit quantities, coarse locations, and inter-object relations. Conventional dense visual-token representations don't align well with these structured semantics, creating an interface mismatch between perception and language reasoning.
AI.method: AeroRAG converts input images into structured visual knowledge (object categories, quantities, spatial locations, semantic relations), then retrieves query-relevant semantic chunks to construct compact prompts for text-based LLMs. This creates an explicit intermediate interface between perception and language reasoning instead of relying on direct reasoning over dense visual tokens.
AI.result: Experiments on AUG aerial dataset and VG-150 benchmark show consistent improvements over six strong MLLM baselines, with largest gains in dense aerial scenes and relation-sensitive reasoning. Framework also performs well on VQAv2, demonstrating compatibility with standard visual reasoning settings.
AI.conclusion: Structured retrieval is a practical design direction for deployment-oriented and grounded visual reasoning systems, providing a more explicit interface between perception and language reasoning that addresses the limitations of conventional dense visual-token representations in aerial scenes.

title: ReTrack: Evidence-Driven Dual-Stream Directional Anchor Calibration Network for Composed Video Retrieval
summary: With the rapid growth of video data, Composed Video Retrieval (CVR) has emerged as a novel paradigm in video retrieval and is receiving increasing attention from researchers. Unlike unimodal video retrieval methods, the CVR task takes a multi-modal query consisting of a reference video and a piece of modification text as input. The modification text conveys the user's intended alterations to the reference video. Based on this input, the model aims to retrieve the most relevant target video. In the CVR task, there exists a substantial discrepancy in information density between video and text modalities. Traditional composition methods tend to bias the composed feature toward the reference video, which leads to suboptimal retrieval performance. This limitation is significant due to the presence of three core challenges: (1) modal contribution entanglement, (2) explicit optimization of composed features, and (3) retrieval uncertainty. To address these challenges, we propose the evidence-dRivRn dual-sTream diRectionAl anChor calibration networK (ReTrack). ReTrack is the first CVR framework that improves multi-modal query understanding by calibrating directional bias in composed features. It consists of three key modules: Semantic Contribution Disentanglement, Composition Geometry Calibration, and Reliable Evidence-driven Alignment. Specifically, ReTrack estimates the semantic contribution of each modality to calibrate the directional bias of the composed feature. It then uses the calibrated directional anchors to compute bidirectional evidence that drives reliable composed-to-target similarity estimation. Moreover, ReTrack exhibits strong generalization to the Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) task, achieving SOTA performance across three benchmark datasets in both CVR and CIR scenarios. Codes are available at https://github.com/Lee-zixu/ReTrack

AI.tldr: ReTrack is a novel framework for Composed Video Retrieval that addresses modality bias by calibrating directional anchors in composed features, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional CVR methods suffer from bias toward reference videos due to information density discrepancy between video and text modalities, leading to suboptimal retrieval performance. Three core challenges exist: modal contribution entanglement, explicit optimization of composed features, and retrieval uncertainty.
AI.method: ReTrack uses three key modules: Semantic Contribution Disentanglement to estimate modality contributions, Composition Geometry Calibration to adjust directional bias, and Reliable Evidence-driven Alignment that computes bidirectional evidence for similarity estimation.
AI.result: ReTrack achieves state-of-the-art performance across three benchmark datasets in both Composed Video Retrieval and Composed Image Retrieval scenarios, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: ReTrack is the first CVR framework to address directional bias in composed features through evidence-driven calibration, significantly improving multi-modal query understanding and retrieval performance while generalizing well to related tasks.

title: MEDN: Motion-Emotion Feature Decoupling Network for Micro-Expression Recognition
summary: Unlike macro-expression, micro-expression does not follow a strictly consistent mapping rule between emotions and Action Units (AUs). As a result, some micro-expressions share identical AUs yet represent completely opposite emotional categories, making them highly visually similar. Existing microexpression recognition (MER) methods mostly rely on explicit facial motion cues (e.g., optical flow, frame differences, AU features) while ignoring implicit emotion information. To tackle this issue, this paper presents a Motion Emotion Feature Decoupling Network (MEDN) for MER. We design a dual-branch framework to separately extract motion and emotion features. In the motion branch, an AU-detection task restricts features to the explicit motion domain, and orthogonal loss is adopted to reduce motion emotion feature coupling. For implicit emotion modeling, we propose a Sparse Emotion Vision Transformer (SEVit) that sparsifies spatial tokens to highlight local temporal variations with multi-scale sparsity rates. A Collaborative Fusion Module (CoFM) is further developed to fuse disentangled motion and emotion features adaptively. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets validate that MEDN effectively decouples motion and emotion features and achieves superior recognition performance, offering a new perspective for enhancing recognition accuracy and generalization.

AI.tldr: MEDN: A dual-branch network that decouples motion and emotion features for micro-expression recognition, addressing the challenge of visually similar expressions with identical AUs but opposite emotions.
AI.motivation: Micro-expressions don't follow consistent emotion-AU mapping rules, causing identical AUs to represent opposite emotions. Existing methods rely on explicit facial motion cues while ignoring implicit emotion information, limiting recognition accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes Motion Emotion Feature Decoupling Network (MEDN) with dual branches: motion branch uses AU-detection task and orthogonal loss to extract explicit motion features; emotion branch uses Sparse Emotion Vision Transformer (SEVit) with multi-scale sparsity rates to highlight local temporal variations. Features are fused via Collaborative Fusion Module (CoFM).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets show MEDN effectively decouples motion and emotion features and achieves superior recognition performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: MEDN offers a new perspective for enhancing micro-expression recognition accuracy and generalization by explicitly decoupling motion and emotion features, addressing the fundamental challenge of ambiguous emotion-AU mappings in micro-expressions.

title: Beyond Binary Contrast: Modeling Continuous Skeleton Action Spaces with Transitional Anchors
summary: Self-supervised contrastive learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for skeleton-based action recognition by enforcing consistency in the embedding space. However, existing methods rely on binary contrastive objectives that overlook the intrinsic continuity of human motion, resulting in fragmented feature clusters and rigid class boundaries. To address these limitations, we propose TranCLR, a Transitional anchor-based Contrastive Learning framework that captures the continuous geometry of the action space. Specifically, the proposed Action Transitional Anchor Construction (ATAC) explicitly models the geometric structure of transitional states to enhance the model's perception of motion continuity. Building upon these anchors, a Multi-Level Geometric Manifold Calibration (MGMC) mechanism is introduced to adaptively calibrate the action manifold across multiple levels of continuity, yielding a smoother and more discriminative representation space. Extensive experiments on the NTU RGB+D, NTU RGB+D 120 and PKU-MMD datasets demonstrate that TranCLR achieves superior accuracy and calibration performance, effectively learning continuous and uncertainty-aware skeleton representations. The code is available at https://github.com/Philchieh/TranCLR.

AI.tldr: TranCLR introduces transitional anchor-based contrastive learning for skeleton action recognition, addressing motion continuity issues in existing binary contrastive methods through geometric modeling and manifold calibration.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised contrastive learning methods for skeleton-based action recognition use binary contrastive objectives that fail to capture the intrinsic continuity of human motion, leading to fragmented feature clusters and rigid class boundaries.
AI.method: Proposes TranCLR with two key components: 1) Action Transitional Anchor Construction (ATAC) that explicitly models geometric structure of transitional states, and 2) Multi-Level Geometric Manifold Calibration (MGMC) that adaptively calibrates the action manifold across multiple continuity levels.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on NTU RGB+D, NTU RGB+D 120, and PKU-MMD datasets show TranCLR achieves superior accuracy and calibration performance, effectively learning continuous and uncertainty-aware skeleton representations.
AI.conclusion: TranCLR successfully addresses the limitations of binary contrastive learning by capturing motion continuity through transitional anchors and geometric manifold calibration, resulting in smoother and more discriminative representation spaces for skeleton-based action recognition.

title: OneDrive: Unified Multi-Paradigm Driving with Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language Models(VLMs) excel at autoregressive text generation, yet end-to-end autonomous driving requires multi-task learning with structured outputs and heterogeneous decoding behaviors, such as autoregressive language generation, parallel object detection and trajectory regression. To accommodate these differences, existing systems typically introduce separate or cascaded decoders, resulting in architectural fragmentation and limited backbone reuse. In this work, we present a unified autonomous driving framework built upon a pretrained VLM, where heterogeneous decoding behaviors are reconciled within a single transformer decoder. We demonstrate that pretrained VLM attention exhibits strong transferability beyond pure language modeling. By organizing visual and structured query tokens within a single causal decoder, structured queries can naturally condition on visual context through the original attention mechanism. Textual and structured outputs share a common attention backbone, enabling stable joint optimization across heterogeneous tasks. Trajectory planning is realized within the same causal LLM decoder by introducing structured trajectory queries. This unified formulation enables planning to share the pretrained attention backbone with images and perception tokens. Extensive experiments on end-to-end autonomous driving benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, including 0.28 L2 and 0.18 collision rate on nuScenes open-loop evaluation and competitive results (86.8 PDMS) on NAVSIM closed-loop evaluation. The full model preserves multi-modal generation capability, while an efficient inference mode achieves approximately 40% lower latency. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Z1zyw/OneDrive

AI.tldr: OneDrive: A unified autonomous driving framework using a single transformer decoder from pretrained VLM to handle heterogeneous tasks (language, perception, planning) with shared attention backbone.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous driving systems use separate/cascaded decoders for different tasks (language generation, object detection, trajectory regression), causing architectural fragmentation and limited backbone reuse. Need to reconcile heterogeneous decoding behaviors within a unified framework.
AI.method: Build upon pretrained VLM with single transformer decoder. Organize visual and structured query tokens within causal decoder. Use original attention mechanism for structured queries to condition on visual context. Introduce structured trajectory queries for planning within same LLM decoder. Share attention backbone across textual and structured outputs.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance: 0.28 L2 and 0.18 collision rate on nuScenes open-loop evaluation, competitive 86.8 PDMS on NAVSIM closed-loop evaluation. Efficient inference mode achieves ~40% lower latency. Full model preserves multi-modal generation capability.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained VLM attention transfers well beyond language modeling. Single decoder can handle heterogeneous autonomous driving tasks with stable joint optimization. Unified formulation enables planning to share pretrained attention backbone with perception and images.

title: Prompting Foundation Models for Zero-Shot Ship Instance Segmentation in SAR Imagery
summary: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) plays a critical role in maritime surveillance, yet deep learning for SAR analysis is limited by the lack of pixel-level annotations. This paper explores how general-purpose vision foundation models can enable zero-shot ship instance segmentation in SAR imagery, eliminating the need for pixel-level supervision. A YOLOv11-based detector trained on open SAR datasets localizes ships via bounding boxes, which then prompt the Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) to produce instance masks without any mask annotations. Unlike prior SAM-based SAR approaches that rely on fine tuning or adapters, our method demonstrates that spatial constraints from a SAR-trained detector alone can effectively regularize foundation model predictions. This design partially mitigates the optical-SAR domain gap and enables downstream applications such as vessel classification, size estimation, and wake analysis. Experiments on the SSDD benchmark achieve a mean IoU of 0.637 (89% of a fully supervised baseline) with an overall ship detection rate of 89.2%, confirming a scalable, annotation-efficient pathway toward foundation-model-driven SAR image understanding.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot ship instance segmentation in SAR imagery using YOLOv11 for detection and SAM2 for segmentation without pixel-level annotations, achieving 89% of supervised baseline performance.
AI.motivation: Deep learning for SAR analysis is limited by lack of pixel-level annotations. The paper aims to enable ship instance segmentation in SAR imagery without requiring expensive mask annotations by leveraging general-purpose vision foundation models.
AI.method: Uses YOLOv11-based detector trained on open SAR datasets to localize ships via bounding boxes, then prompts Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) to produce instance masks without any mask annotations. Unlike prior approaches, relies only on spatial constraints from SAR-trained detector to regularize foundation model predictions.
AI.result: Achieves mean IoU of 0.637 (89% of a fully supervised baseline) on SSDD benchmark with overall ship detection rate of 89.2%. Demonstrates effective mitigation of optical-SAR domain gap and enables downstream applications like vessel classification, size estimation, and wake analysis.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a scalable, annotation-efficient pathway toward foundation-model-driven SAR image understanding by showing that spatial constraints from a SAR-trained detector alone can effectively regularize foundation model predictions without fine-tuning or adapters.

title: Brain-Inspired Capture: Evidence-Driven Neuromimetic Perceptual Simulation for Visual Decoding
summary: Visual decoding of neurophysiological signals is a critical challenge for brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) and computational neuroscience. However, current approaches are often constrained by the systematic and stochastic gaps between neural and visual modalities, largely neglecting the intrinsic computational mechanisms of the Human Visual System (HVS). To address this, we propose Brain-Inspired Capture (BI-Cap), a neuromimetic perceptual simulation paradigm that aligns these modalities by emulating HVS processing. Specifically, we construct a neuromimetic pipeline comprising four biologically plausible dynamic and static transformations, coupled with Mutual Information (MI)-guided dynamic blur regulation to simulate adaptive visual processing. Furthermore, to mitigate the inherent non-stationarity of neural activity, we introduce an evidence-driven latent space representation. This formulation explicitly models uncertainty, thereby ensuring robust neural embeddings. Extensive evaluations on zero-shot brain-to-image retrieval across two public benchmarks demonstrate that BI-Cap substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving relative gains of 9.2\% and 8.0\%, respectively. We have released the source code on GitHub through the link https://github.com/flysnow1024/BI-Cap.

AI.tldr: BI-Cap is a brain-inspired perceptual simulation paradigm that aligns neural and visual modalities by emulating Human Visual System processing, achieving state-of-the-art performance in zero-shot brain-to-image retrieval.
AI.motivation: Current brain-computer interface approaches have systematic gaps between neural and visual modalities and neglect the intrinsic computational mechanisms of the Human Visual System, limiting their effectiveness.
AI.method: Proposes a neuromimetic pipeline with four biologically plausible transformations, Mutual Information-guided dynamic blur regulation to simulate adaptive visual processing, and an evidence-driven latent space representation to handle neural non-stationarity and uncertainty.
AI.result: BI-Cap substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods on zero-shot brain-to-image retrieval across two public benchmarks, achieving relative gains of 9.2% and 8.0% respectively.
AI.conclusion: The brain-inspired approach that emulates Human Visual System processing effectively bridges the gap between neural and visual modalities, offering a promising direction for brain-computer interfaces and computational neuroscience.

title: From Heads to Neurons: Causal Attribution and Steering in Multi-Task Vision-Language Models
summary: Recent work has increasingly explored neuron-level interpretation in vision-language models (VLMs) to identify neurons critical to final predictions. However, existing neuron analyses generally focus on single tasks, limiting the comparability of neuron importance across tasks. Moreover, ranking strategies tend to score neurons in isolation, overlooking how task-dependent information pathways shape the write-in effects of feed-forward network (FFN) neurons. This oversight can exacerbate neuron polysemanticity in multi-task settings, introducing noise into the identification and intervention of task-critical neurons. In this study, we propose HONES (Head-Oriented Neuron Explanation & Steering), a gradient-free framework for task-aware neuron attribution and steering in multi-task VLMs. HONES ranks FFN neurons by their causal write-in contributions conditioned on task-relevant attention heads, and further modulates salient neurons via lightweight scaling. Experiments on four diverse multimodal tasks and two popular VLMs show that HONES outperforms existing methods in identifying task-critical neurons and improves model performance after steering. Our source code is released at: https://github.com/petergit1/HONES.

AI.tldr: HONES is a gradient-free framework for task-aware neuron attribution and steering in multi-task vision-language models that ranks FFN neurons by their causal write-in contributions conditioned on task-relevant attention heads.
AI.motivation: Existing neuron interpretation methods focus on single tasks, limiting comparability across tasks, and rank neurons in isolation, overlooking how task-dependent information pathways shape FFN neuron effects, which exacerbates neuron polysemanticity in multi-task settings.
AI.method: HONES uses a gradient-free framework that ranks feed-forward network (FFN) neurons by their causal write-in contributions conditioned on task-relevant attention heads, and modulates salient neurons via lightweight scaling.
AI.result: Experiments on four diverse multimodal tasks and two popular VLMs show that HONES outperforms existing methods in identifying task-critical neurons and improves model performance after steering.
AI.conclusion: HONES provides an effective framework for task-aware neuron attribution and steering in multi-task VLMs, addressing limitations of existing single-task focused approaches and improving both neuron identification and model performance.

title: ZSG-IAD: A Multimodal Framework for Zero-Shot Grounded Industrial Anomaly Detection
summary: Deep learning-based industrial anomaly detectors often behave as black boxes, making it hard to justify decisions with physically meaningful defect evidence. We propose ZSG-IAD, a multimodal vision-language framework for zero-shot grounded industrial anomaly detection. Given RGB images, sensor images, and 3D point clouds, ZSG-IAD generates structured anomaly reports and pixel-level anomaly masks. ZSG-IAD introduces a language-guided two-hop grounding module: (1) anomaly-related sentences select evidence-like latent slots distilled from multimodal features, yielding coarse spatial support; (2) selected slots modulate feature maps via channel-spatial gating and a lightweight decoder to produce fine-grained masks. To improve reliability, we further apply Executable-Rule GRPO with verifiable rewards to promote structured outputs, anomaly-region consistency, and reasoning-conclusion coherence. Experiments across multiple industrial anomaly benchmarks show strong zero-shot performance and more transparent, physically grounded explanations than prior methods. We will release code and annotations to support future research on trustworthy industrial anomaly detection systems.

AI.tldr: ZSG-IAD is a multimodal vision-language framework for zero-shot grounded industrial anomaly detection that generates structured anomaly reports and pixel-level masks using language-guided grounding and executable-rule reinforcement learning.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning-based industrial anomaly detectors act as black boxes, lacking physically meaningful defect evidence and transparent justification for their decisions.
AI.method: Uses multimodal inputs (RGB images, sensor images, 3D point clouds) with a language-guided two-hop grounding module: 1) anomaly-related sentences select evidence-like latent slots from multimodal features for coarse spatial support, 2) selected slots modulate feature maps via channel-spatial gating and lightweight decoder for fine-grained masks. Applies Executable-Rule GRPO with verifiable rewards to improve reliability.
AI.result: Shows strong zero-shot performance across multiple industrial anomaly benchmarks and produces more transparent, physically grounded explanations than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: ZSG-IAD enables trustworthy industrial anomaly detection with structured outputs and physically meaningful explanations, advancing research on transparent AI systems for industrial applications.

title: Chatting about Upper-Body Expressive Human Pose and Shape Estimation
summary: Expressive Human Pose and Shape Estimation (EHPS) plays a crucial role in various AR/VR applications and has witnessed significant progress in recent years. However, current state-of-the-art methods still struggle with accurate parameter estimation for facial and hand regions and exhibit limited generalization to wild images. To address these challenges, we present CoEvoer, a novel one-stage synergistic cross-dependency transformer framework tailored for upper-body EHPS. CoEvoer enables explicit feature-level interaction across different body parts, allowing for mutual enhancement through contextual information exchange. Specifically, larger and more easily estimated regions such as the torso provide global semantics and positional priors to guide the estimation of finer, more complex regions like the face and hands. Conversely, the localized details captured in facial and hand regions help refine and calibrate adjacent body parts. To the best of our knowledge, CoEvoer is the first framework designed specifically for upper-body EHPS, with the goal of capturing the strong coupling and semantic dependencies among the face, hands, and torso through joint parameter regression. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoEvoer achieves state-of-the-art performance on upper-body benchmarks and exhibits strong generalization capability even on unseen wild images.

AI.tldr: CoEvoer is a novel one-stage transformer framework for upper-body expressive human pose and shape estimation that enables explicit feature-level interaction across body parts to improve accuracy and generalization.
AI.motivation: Current state-of-the-art methods struggle with accurate facial and hand parameter estimation and have limited generalization to wild images. There's a need for better modeling of dependencies between different upper-body regions.
AI.method: CoEvoer uses a synergistic cross-dependency transformer framework that enables explicit feature-level interaction across body parts. Larger regions (torso) provide global semantics and positional priors to guide finer regions (face, hands), while localized details from face/hands refine adjacent parts through joint parameter regression.
AI.result: CoEvoer achieves state-of-the-art performance on upper-body benchmarks and exhibits strong generalization capability even on unseen wild images.
AI.conclusion: CoEvoer is the first framework specifically designed for upper-body EHPS that effectively captures the strong coupling and semantic dependencies among face, hands, and torso, addressing key limitations in current methods.

title: DifFoundMAD: Foundation Models meet Differential Morphing Attack Detection
summary: In this work, we introduce DifFoundMAD, a parameter-efficient D-MAD framework that exploits the generalisation capabilities of vision foundation models (FM) to capture discrepancies between suspected morphs and live capture images. In contrast to conventional D-MAD systems that rely on face recognition embeddings or handcrafted feature differences, DifFoundMAD follows the standard differential paradigm while replacing the underlying representation space with embeddings extracted from FMs. By combining lightweight finetuning with class-balanced optimisation, the proposed method updates only a small subset of parameters while preserving the rich representational priors of the underlying FMs. Extensive cross-database evaluations on standard D-MAD benchmarks demonstrate that DifFoundMAD achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art systems, particularly at the strict security levels required in operational deployments such as border control: The error rates reported in the current state-of-the-art were reduced from 6.16% to 2.17% for high-security levels using DifFoundMAD.

AI.tldr: DifFoundMAD is a parameter-efficient differential morphing attack detection framework that uses vision foundation model embeddings instead of traditional face recognition features, achieving significant error rate reductions at high-security levels.
AI.motivation: Current D-MAD systems rely on face recognition embeddings or handcrafted features, which may not generalize well. The paper aims to leverage the superior generalization capabilities of vision foundation models to better detect morphing attacks, especially for critical applications like border control.
AI.method: DifFoundMAD follows the differential paradigm but replaces the underlying representation space with embeddings from vision foundation models. It uses lightweight fine-tuning with class-balanced optimization, updating only a small subset of parameters while preserving the FM's rich representational priors.
AI.result: Extensive cross-database evaluations show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art systems. Most notably, error rates at high-security levels were reduced from 6.16% to 2.17% - a significant improvement for operational deployments like border control.
AI.conclusion: DifFoundMAD demonstrates that vision foundation models provide superior representations for D-MAD tasks, and that parameter-efficient fine-tuning with class-balanced optimization can effectively leverage these models while maintaining their generalization capabilities.

title: MU-GeNeRF: Multi-view Uncertainty-guided Generalizable Neural Radiance Fields for Distractor-aware Scene
summary: Generalizable Neural Radiance Fields (GeNeRFs) enable high-quality scene reconstruction from sparse views and can generalize to unseen scenes. However, in real-world settings, transient distractors break cross-view structural consistency, corrupting supervision and degrading reconstruction quality. Existing distractor-free NeRF methods rely on per-scene optimization and estimate uncertainty from per-view reconstruction errors, which are not reliable for GeNeRFs and often misjudge inconsistent static structures as distractors. To this end, we propose MU-GeNeRF, a Multi-view Uncertainty-guided distractor-aware GeNeRF framework designed to alleviate GeNeRF's robust modeling challenges in the presence of transient distractions. We decompose distractor awareness into two complementary uncertainty components: Source-view Uncertainty, which captures structural discrepancies across source views caused by viewpoint changes or dynamic factors; and Target-view Uncertainty, which detects observation anomalies in the target image induced by transient distractors.These two uncertainties address distinct error sources and are combined through a heteroscedastic reconstruction loss, which guides the model to adaptively modulate supervision, enabling more robust distractor suppression and geometric modeling.Extensive experiments show that our method not only surpasses existing GeNeRFs but also achieves performance comparable to scene-specific distractor-free NeRFs.

AI.tldr: MU-GeNeRF improves generalizable NeRF robustness to transient distractors by combining source-view and target-view uncertainty estimation with heteroscedastic reconstruction loss.
AI.motivation: Transient distractors in real-world scenes break cross-view consistency, corrupting supervision and degrading reconstruction quality in generalizable NeRFs. Existing distractor-free methods rely on per-scene optimization and unreliable uncertainty estimation that often misclassifies static structures as distractors.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-view uncertainty-guided framework with two complementary uncertainty components: source-view uncertainty (captures structural discrepancies across views) and target-view uncertainty (detects observation anomalies in target images). These are combined through a heteroscedastic reconstruction loss that adaptively modulates supervision.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the method surpasses existing generalizable NeRFs and achieves performance comparable to scene-specific distractor-free NeRFs.
AI.conclusion: MU-GeNeRF effectively addresses robust modeling challenges in generalizable NeRFs by decomposing distractor awareness into complementary uncertainty components, enabling more reliable distractor suppression and geometric reconstruction.

title: E3VS-Bench: A Benchmark for Viewpoint-Dependent Active Perception in 3D Gaussian Splatting Scenes
summary: Visual search in 3D environments requires embodied agents to actively explore their surroundings and acquire task-relevant evidence. However, existing visual search and embodied AI benchmarks, including EQA, typically rely on static observations or constrained egocentric motion, and thus do not explicitly evaluate fine-grained viewpoint-dependent phenomena that arise under unrestricted 5-DoF viewpoint control in real-world 3D environments, such as visibility changes caused by vertical viewpoint shifts, revealing contents inside containers, and disambiguating object attributes that are only observable from specific angles. To address this limitation, we introduce {E3VS-Bench}, a benchmark for embodied 3D visual search where agents must control their viewpoints in 5-DoF to gather viewpoint-dependent evidence for question answering. E3VS-Bench consists of 99 high-fidelity 3D scenes reconstructed using 3D Gaussian Splatting and 2,014 question-driven episodes. 3D Gaussian Splatting enables photorealistic free-viewpoint rendering that preserves fine-grained visual details (e.g., small text and subtle attributes) often degraded in mesh-based simulators, thereby allowing the construction of questions that cannot be answered from a single view and instead require active inspection across viewpoints in 5-DoF. We evaluate multiple state-of-the-art VLMs and compare their performance with humans. Despite strong 2D reasoning ability, all models exhibit a substantial gap from humans, highlighting limitations in active perception and coherent viewpoint planning specifically under full 5-DoF viewpoint changes.

AI.tldr: E3VS-Bench: A new benchmark for embodied 3D visual search requiring 5-DoF viewpoint control to gather evidence for question answering, using 3D Gaussian Splatting for photorealistic rendering.
AI.motivation: Existing visual search benchmarks rely on static observations or constrained motion, failing to evaluate fine-grained viewpoint-dependent phenomena in real-world 3D environments like visibility changes from vertical shifts, revealing container contents, and disambiguating object attributes from specific angles.
AI.method: Created E3VS-Bench with 99 high-fidelity 3D scenes reconstructed using 3D Gaussian Splatting and 2,014 question-driven episodes. 3D Gaussian Splatting enables photorealistic free-viewpoint rendering preserving fine visual details that mesh-based simulators degrade.
AI.result: Evaluated multiple state-of-the-art VLMs and compared with humans. Despite strong 2D reasoning ability, all models show substantial gap from humans, highlighting limitations in active perception and coherent viewpoint planning under full 5-DoF viewpoint changes.
AI.conclusion: E3VS-Bench addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by enabling evaluation of embodied agents' ability to actively control viewpoints in 5-DoF for visual search tasks, revealing significant gaps between current models and human performance in active perception and viewpoint planning.

title: Identifying Ethical Biases in Action Recognition Models
summary: Human Action Recognition (HAR) models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, yet their fairness across different human appearances has not been analyzed. We introduce a framework for auditing bias in HAR models using synthetic video data, generated with full control over visual identity attributes such as skin color. Unlike prior work that focuses on static images or pose estimation, our approach preserves temporal consistency, allowing us to isolate and test how changes to a single attribute affect model predictions. Through controlled interventions using the BEDLAM simulation platform, we show whether some popular HAR models exhibit statistically significant biases on the skin color even when the motion remains identical. Our results highlight how models may encode unwanted visual associations, and we provide evidence of systematic errors across groups. This work contributes a framework for auditing HAR models and supports the development of more transparent, accountable systems in light of upcoming regulatory standards.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework for auditing bias in Human Action Recognition models using synthetic video data with controlled identity attributes like skin color, revealing systematic biases even when motion remains identical.
AI.motivation: HAR models are deployed in high-stakes environments but their fairness across different human appearances hasn't been analyzed. There's a need to audit these models for bias, especially as regulatory standards are emerging.
AI.method: Uses synthetic video data generated with full control over visual identity attributes (skin color) via BEDLAM simulation platform. Preserves temporal consistency to isolate how single attribute changes affect predictions, unlike prior work on static images or pose estimation.
AI.result: Shows that popular HAR models exhibit statistically significant biases based on skin color even when motion remains identical. Provides evidence of systematic errors across groups and unwanted visual associations encoded in models.
AI.conclusion: Contributes a framework for auditing HAR models and supports development of more transparent, accountable systems in preparation for upcoming regulatory standards on fairness and bias.

title: Mitigating Multimodal Hallucination via Phase-wise Self-reward
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) still struggle with vision hallucination, where generated responses are inconsistent with the visual input. Existing methods either rely on large-scale annotated data for fine-tuning, which incurs massive computational overhead, or employ static post-hoc strategies that overlook the dynamic nature of hallucination emergence. To address these, we introduce a new self-rewarding framework, enabling dynamic hallucination mitigation at inference time without external supervision. On the empirical side, we reveal that visual hallucination exhibits phase-wise dynamic patterns, peaking at the onset of each semantic phase. Drawing on these insights, we propose \textbf{PSRD} (\textbf{Phase-wise \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{R}eward \textbf{D}ecoding) for online hallucination correction guided by phase-wise self-reward signals. To reduce the cost of repeated self-evaluation during decoding, we distill the hallucination guidance signal from LVLMs into a lightweight reward model. The reward model subsequently provides on-the-fly guidance for targeted intervention during the decoding process, enabling precise hallucination suppression. The proposed PSRD significantly reduces the hallucination rate of LLaVA-1.5-7B by 50.0% and consistently outperforms existing post-hoc methods across five hallucination evaluation benchmarks for four LVLMs. Further analysis confirms that PSRD effectively mitigates hallucination propagation and achieves a highly controllable trade-off between strong performance and inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: PSRD is a self-rewarding framework that dynamically mitigates vision hallucination in LVLMs at inference time without external supervision, using phase-wise self-reward signals and a distilled lightweight reward model.
AI.motivation: LVLMs suffer from vision hallucination where responses don't match visual input. Existing methods require expensive fine-tuning or use static post-hoc strategies that ignore the dynamic nature of hallucination emergence.
AI.method: PSRD (Phase-wise Self-Reward Decoding) uses phase-wise self-reward signals for online hallucination correction. A lightweight reward model is distilled from LVLMs to provide on-the-fly guidance during decoding, enabling precise hallucination suppression without repeated self-evaluation.
AI.result: PSRD reduces hallucination rate of LLaVA-1.5-7B by 50.0% and consistently outperforms existing post-hoc methods across five hallucination evaluation benchmarks for four LVLMs. It effectively mitigates hallucination propagation and achieves controllable trade-off between performance and inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed self-rewarding framework enables dynamic hallucination mitigation at inference time without external supervision, offering an efficient and effective solution to vision hallucination in LVLMs.

title: Trustworthy Endoscopic Super-Resolution
summary: Super-resolution (SR) models are attracting growing interest for enhancing minimally invasive surgery and diagnostic videos under hardware constraints. However, valid concerns remain regarding the introduction of hallucinated structures and amplified noise, limiting their reliability in safety-critical settings. We propose a direct and practical framework to make SR systems more trustworthy by identifying where reconstructions are likely to fail. Our approach integrates a lightweight error-prediction network that operates on intermediate representations to estimate pixel-wise reconstruction error. The module is computationally efficient and low-latency, making it suitable for real-time deployment. We convert these predictions into operational failure decisions by constructing Conformal Failure Masks (CFM), which localize regions where the SR output should not be trusted. Built on conformal risk control principles, our method provides theoretical guarantees for controlling both the tolerated error limit and the miscoverage in detected failures. We evaluate our approach on image and video SR, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting unreliable reconstructions in endoscopic and robotic surgery settings. To our knowledge, this is the first study to provide a model-agnostic, theoretically grounded approach to improving the safety of real-time endoscopic image SR.

AI.tldr: A framework for making super-resolution systems more trustworthy by predicting reconstruction errors and generating conformal failure masks with theoretical guarantees for safety-critical medical applications.
AI.motivation: Super-resolution models for medical imaging (endoscopic/robotic surgery) face reliability concerns due to hallucinated structures and amplified noise, limiting their use in safety-critical settings where trustworthiness is essential.
AI.method: Integrates a lightweight error-prediction network that operates on intermediate representations to estimate pixel-wise reconstruction error. Converts predictions into Conformal Failure Masks (CFM) using conformal risk control principles to localize unreliable regions with theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness in detecting unreliable reconstructions in endoscopic and robotic surgery settings for both image and video super-resolution. The approach is computationally efficient and suitable for real-time deployment.
AI.conclusion: First model-agnostic, theoretically grounded approach to improve safety of real-time endoscopic image super-resolution, providing a practical framework for making SR systems more trustworthy in medical applications.

title: Multi-View Hierarchical Graph Neural Network for Sketch-Based 3D Shape Retrieval
summary: Sketch-based 3D shape retrieval (SBSR) aims to retrieve 3D shapes that are consistent with the category of the input hand-drawn sketch. The core challenge of this task lies in two aspects: existing methods typically employ simplified aggregation strategies for independently encoded 3D multi-view features, which ignore the geometric relationships between views and multi-level details, resulting in weak 3D representation. Simultaneously, traditional SBSR methods are constrained by visible category limitations, leading to poor performance in zero-shot scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose Multi-View Hierarchical Graph Neural Network (MV-HGNN), a novel framework for SBSR. Specifically, we construct a view-level graph and capture adjacent geometric dependencies and cross-view message passing via local graph convolution and global attention. A view selector is further introduced to perform hierarchical graph coarsening, enabling a progressively larger receptive field for graph convolution and mitigating the interference of redundant views, which leads to more discriminate discriminative hierarchical 3D representation. To enable category agnostic alignment and mitigate overfitting to seen classes, we leverage CLIP text embeddings as semantic prototypes and project both sketch and 3D features into a shared semantic space. We use a two-stage training strategy for category-level retrieval and a one-stage strategy for zero-shot retrieval under the same model architecture. Under both category-level and zero-shot settings, extensive experiments on two public benchmarks demonstrate that MV-HGNN outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: MV-HGNN: A novel framework for sketch-based 3D shape retrieval using multi-view hierarchical graph neural networks with CLIP semantic alignment, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both category-level and zero-shot settings.
AI.motivation: Existing SBSR methods have two main limitations: 1) They use simplified aggregation strategies for 3D multi-view features that ignore geometric relationships between views and multi-level details, resulting in weak 3D representation. 2) Traditional methods are constrained by visible category limitations, leading to poor performance in zero-shot scenarios.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-View Hierarchical Graph Neural Network (MV-HGNN) with: 1) View-level graph construction capturing adjacent geometric dependencies and cross-view message passing via local graph convolution and global attention. 2) View selector for hierarchical graph coarsening to increase receptive field and reduce redundant view interference. 3) CLIP text embeddings as semantic prototypes to project sketch and 3D features into shared semantic space for category-agnostic alignment. Uses two-stage training for category-level retrieval and one-stage for zero-shot retrieval.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two public benchmarks show MV-HGNN outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both category-level and zero-shot settings.
AI.conclusion: MV-HGNN effectively addresses the limitations of existing SBSR methods by capturing geometric relationships between views through hierarchical graph neural networks and enabling zero-shot capability through CLIP-based semantic alignment, demonstrating superior performance across different retrieval scenarios.

title: CFSR: Geometry-Conditioned Shadow Removal via Physical Disentanglement
summary: Traditional shadow removal networks often treat image restoration as an unconstrained mapping, lacking the physical interpretability required to balance localized texture recovery with global illumination consistency. To address this, we propose CFSR, a multi-modal prior-driven framework that reframes shadow removal as a physics-constrained restoration process. By seamlessly integrating 3D geometric cues with large-scale foundation model semantics, CFSR effectively bridges the 2D-3D domain gap. Specifically, we first map observations into a custom HVI color space to suppress shadow-induced noise and robustly fuse RGB data with estimated depth priors. At its core, our Geometric & Semantic Dual Explicit Guided Attention mechanism utilizes DINO features and 3D surface normals to directly modulate the attention affinity matrix, structurally enforcing physical lighting constraints. To recover severely degraded regions, we inject holistic priors via a frozen CLIP encoder. Finally, our Frequency Collaborative Reconstruction Module (FCRM) achieves an optimal synthesis by decoupling the decoding process. Conditioned on geometric priors, FCRM seamlessly harmonizes the reconstruction of sharp high-frequency occlusion boundaries with the restoration of low-frequency global illumination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CFSR achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple challenging benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CFSR is a physics-constrained shadow removal framework that integrates 3D geometric cues with foundation model semantics to bridge the 2D-3D domain gap, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional shadow removal networks lack physical interpretability and struggle to balance localized texture recovery with global illumination consistency, treating restoration as unconstrained mapping without physical constraints.
AI.method: Proposes CFSR with: 1) HVI color space mapping to suppress shadow noise and fuse RGB with depth priors; 2) Geometric & Semantic Dual Explicit Guided Attention using DINO features and 3D normals to modulate attention affinity matrix; 3) Holistic priors injection via frozen CLIP encoder; 4) Frequency Collaborative Reconstruction Module (FCRM) that decouples decoding to harmonize high-frequency boundaries with low-frequency illumination.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CFSR achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple challenging benchmarks, demonstrating superior shadow removal capabilities.
AI.conclusion: CFSR successfully reframes shadow removal as a physics-constrained restoration process by integrating 3D geometric cues with foundation model semantics, effectively bridging the 2D-3D domain gap and achieving optimal balance between local texture recovery and global illumination consistency.

title: HABIT: Chrono-Synergia Robust Progressive Learning Framework for Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is a flexible image retrieval paradigm that enables users to accurately locate the target image through a multimodal query composed of a reference image and modification text. Although this task has demonstrated promising applications in personalized search and recommendation systems, it encounters a severe challenge in practical scenarios known as the Noise Triplet Correspondence (NTC) problem. This issue primarily arises from the high cost and subjectivity involved in annotating triplet data. To address this problem, we identify two central challenges: the precise estimation of composed semantic discrepancy and the insufficient progressive adaptation to modification discrepancy. To tackle these challenges, we propose a cHrono-synergiA roBust progressIve learning framework for composed image reTrieval (HABIT), which consists of two core modules. First, the Mutual Knowledge Estimation Module quantifies sample cleanliness by calculating the Transition Rate of mutual information between the composed feature and the target image, thereby effectively identifying clean samples that align with the intended modification semantics. Second, the Dual-consistency Progressive Learning Module introduces a collaborative mechanism between the historical and current models, simulating human habit formation to retain good habits and calibrate bad habits, ultimately enabling robust learning under the presence of NTC. Extensive experiments conducted on two standard CIR datasets demonstrate that HABIT significantly outperforms most methods under various noise ratios, exhibiting superior robustness and retrieval performance. Codes are available at https://github.com/Lee-zixu/HABIT

AI.tldr: HABIT is a robust progressive learning framework for composed image retrieval that addresses the Noise Triplet Correspondence problem through mutual knowledge estimation and dual-consistency progressive learning.
AI.motivation: Composed Image Retrieval faces practical challenges due to Noise Triplet Correspondence (NTC) problem caused by expensive and subjective triplet annotation, leading to noisy training data.
AI.method: Proposes HABIT framework with two modules: 1) Mutual Knowledge Estimation Module quantifies sample cleanliness via Transition Rate of mutual information; 2) Dual-consistency Progressive Learning Module uses historical-current model collaboration to simulate human habit formation for robust learning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two standard CIR datasets show HABIT significantly outperforms most methods under various noise ratios, demonstrating superior robustness and retrieval performance.
AI.conclusion: HABIT effectively addresses NTC problem in CIR through noise-aware sample selection and progressive learning, providing a robust solution for practical deployment of composed image retrieval systems.

title: GS-STVSR: Ultra-Efficient Continuous Spatio-Temporal Video Super-Resolution via 2D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Continuous Spatio-Temporal Video Super-Resolution (C-STVSR) aims to simultaneously enhance the spatial resolution and frame rate of videos by arbitrary scale factors, offering greater flexibility than fixed-scale methods that are constrained by predefined upsampling ratios. In recent years, methods based on Implicit Neural Representations (INR) have made significant progress in C-STVSR by learning continuous mappings from spatio-temporal coordinates to pixel values. However, these methods fundamentally rely on dense pixel-wise grid queries, causing computational cost to scale linearly with the number of interpolated frames and severely limiting inference efficiency. We propose GS-STVSR, an ultra-efficient C-STVSR framework based on 2D Gaussian Splatting (2D-GS) that drives the spatiotemporal evolution of Gaussian kernels through continuous motion modeling, bypassing dense grid queries entirely. We exploit the strong temporal stability of covariance parameters for lightweight intermediate fitting, design an optical flow-guided motion module to derive Gaussian position and color at arbitrary time steps, introduce a Covariance resampling alignment module to prevent covariance drift, and propose an adaptive offset window for large-scale motion. Extensive experiments on Vid4, GoPro, and Adobe240 show that GS-STVSR achieves state-of-the-art quality across all benchmarks. Moreover, its inference time remains nearly constant at conventional temporal scales (X2--X8) and delivers over X3 speedup at extreme scales X32, demonstrating strong practical applicability.

AI.tldr: GS-STVSR is an ultra-efficient continuous spatio-temporal video super-resolution framework using 2D Gaussian Splatting that bypasses dense grid queries, achieving state-of-the-art quality with constant inference time and 3x speedup at extreme scales.
AI.motivation: Existing C-STVSR methods based on Implicit Neural Representations suffer from computational inefficiency due to dense pixel-wise grid queries that scale linearly with interpolated frames, severely limiting practical inference efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes GS-STVSR using 2D Gaussian Splatting with continuous motion modeling: exploits temporal stability of covariance for lightweight fitting, optical flow-guided motion module for arbitrary time steps, covariance resampling alignment to prevent drift, and adaptive offset window for large-scale motion.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art quality on Vid4, GoPro, and Adobe240 benchmarks. Inference time remains nearly constant at conventional scales (X2-X8) and delivers over 3x speedup at extreme scale X32.
AI.conclusion: GS-STVSR provides an ultra-efficient C-STVSR framework that overcomes computational limitations of INR-based methods while maintaining high quality, demonstrating strong practical applicability with constant inference time.

title: INTENT: Invariance and Discrimination-aware Noise Mitigation for Robust Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is a challenging image retrieval paradigm that enables to retrieve target images based on multimodal queries consisting of reference images and modification texts. Although substantial progress has been made in recent years, existing methods assume that all samples are correctly matched. However, in real-world scenarios, due to high triplet annotation costs, CIR datasets inevitably contain annotation errors, resulting in incorrectly matched triplets. To address this issue, the problem of Noisy Triplet Correspondence (NTC) has attracted growing attention. We argue that noise in CIR can be categorized into two types: cross-modal correspondence noise and modality-inherent noise. The former arises from mismatches across modalities, whereas the latter originates from intra-modal background interference or visual factors irrelevant to the coarse-grained modification annotations. However, modality-inherent noise is often overlooked, and research on cross-modal correspondence noise remains nascent. To tackle above issues, we propose the Invariance and discrimiNaTion-awarE Noise neTwork (INTENT), comprising two components: Visual Invariant Composition and Bi-Objective Discriminative Learning, specifically designed to handle the two-aspect noise. The former applies causal intervention on the visual side via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to generate intervened composed features, enforcing visual invariance and enabling the model to ignore modality-inherent noise during composition. The latter adopts collaborative optimization with both positive and negative samples, and constructs a scalable decision boundary that dynamically adjusts decisions based on the loyalty degree, enabling robust correspondence discrimination. Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority and robustness of INTENT.

AI.tldr: INTENT addresses noisy triplet correspondence in composed image retrieval by handling both cross-modal correspondence noise and modality-inherent noise through visual invariant composition and bi-objective discriminative learning.
AI.motivation: Real-world CIR datasets contain annotation errors due to high triplet annotation costs, leading to incorrectly matched triplets. Existing methods assume perfect matches, overlooking two types of noise: cross-modal correspondence noise (mismatches across modalities) and modality-inherent noise (intra-modal background interference or irrelevant visual factors).
AI.method: INTENT has two components: 1) Visual Invariant Composition applies causal intervention via Fast Fourier Transform to generate intervened composed features, enforcing visual invariance to ignore modality-inherent noise. 2) Bi-Objective Discriminative Learning uses collaborative optimization with both positive and negative samples, constructing a scalable decision boundary that dynamically adjusts based on loyalty degree for robust correspondence discrimination.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority and robustness of INTENT in handling noisy triplet correspondence.
AI.conclusion: INTENT effectively addresses both cross-modal correspondence noise and modality-inherent noise in CIR, providing a robust solution for real-world scenarios with annotation errors through its dual-component architecture combining visual invariance and discriminative learning.

title: Enhancing Continual Learning of Vision-Language Models via Dynamic Prefix Weighting
comment: CVPR 2026; revised text and figures for improved readability
summary: We investigate recently introduced domain-class incremental learning scenarios for vision-language models (VLMs). Recent works address this challenge using parameter-efficient methods, such as prefix-tuning or adapters, which facilitate model adaptation to downstream tasks by incorporating task-specific information into input tokens through additive vectors. However, previous approaches often normalize the weights of these vectors, disregarding the fact that different input tokens require different degrees of adjustment. To overcome this issue, we propose Dynamic Prefix Weighting (DPW), a framework that dynamically assigns weights to prefixes, complemented by adapters. DPW consists of 1) a gating module that adjusts the weights of each prefix based on the importance of the corresponding input token, and 2) a weighting mechanism that derives adapter output weights as a residual of prefix-tuning weights, ensuring that adapters are utilized only when necessary. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in domain-class incremental learning scenarios for VLMs. The code is available at: https://github.com/YonseiML/dpw.

AI.tldr: DPW introduces dynamic prefix weighting with adapters for domain-class incremental learning in VLMs, outperforming previous methods by adaptively adjusting prefix weights based on token importance.
AI.motivation: Previous parameter-efficient methods for domain-class incremental learning in vision-language models use fixed normalization for prefix weights, ignoring that different input tokens need different degrees of adjustment.
AI.method: Dynamic Prefix Weighting (DPW) framework with: 1) gating module that adjusts prefix weights based on token importance, and 2) weighting mechanism that derives adapter output weights as residual of prefix-tuning weights, ensuring adapters are used only when needed.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in domain-class incremental learning scenarios for vision-language models.
AI.conclusion: DPW effectively addresses the limitation of fixed prefix weighting by dynamically adjusting weights based on token importance, leading to superior performance in incremental learning for VLMs.

title: Class-specific diffusion models improve military object detection in a low-data domain
summary: Diffusion-based image synthesis has emerged as a promising source of synthetic training data for AI-based object detection and classification. In this work, we investigate whether images generated with diffusion can improve military vehicle detection under low-data conditions. We fine-tuned the text-to-image diffusion model FLUX.1 [dev] using LoRA with only 8 or 24 real images per class across 15 vehicle categories, resulting in class-specific diffusion models, which were used to generate new samples from automatically generated text prompts. The same real images were used to fine-tune the RF-DETR detector for a 15-class object detection task. Synthetic datasets generated by the diffusion models were then used to further improve detector performance. Importantly, no additional real data was required, as the generative models leveraged the same limited training samples. FLUX-generated images improved detection performance, particularly in the low-data regime (up to +8.0% mAP$_{50}$ with 8 real samples). To address the limited geometric control of text prompt-based diffusion, we additionally generated structurally guided synthetic data using ControlNet with Canny edge-map conditioning, yielding a FLUX-ControlNet (FLUX-CN) dataset with explicit control over viewpoint and pose. Structural guidance further enhanced performance when data is scarce (+4.1% mAP$_{50}$ with 8 real samples), but no additional benefit was observed when more real data is available. This study demonstrates that object-specific diffusion models are effective for improving military object detection in a low-data domain, and that structural guidance is most beneficial when real data is highly limited. These results highlight generative image data as an alternative to traditional simulation pipelines for the training of military AI systems.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-generated synthetic images improve military vehicle detection in low-data scenarios, with structural guidance via ControlNet providing additional benefits when real data is extremely limited.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether diffusion-generated synthetic images can improve military vehicle detection under low-data conditions, addressing the challenge of limited training data for specialized domains like military applications.
AI.method: Fine-tuned FLUX.1 diffusion model using LoRA with only 8 or 24 real images per class across 15 vehicle categories, generated synthetic data from text prompts. Also created structurally guided synthetic data using ControlNet with Canny edge-map conditioning (FLUX-CN). Used same real images to fine-tune RF-DETR detector and tested with synthetic datasets.
AI.result: FLUX-generated images improved detection performance, especially in low-data regime (up to +8.0% mAP50 with 8 real samples). Structural guidance via FLUX-CN provided additional +4.1% mAP50 improvement with 8 real samples, but no additional benefit with more real data available.
AI.conclusion: Object-specific diffusion models are effective for improving military object detection in low-data domains, with structural guidance most beneficial when real data is highly limited. Generative image data offers an alternative to traditional simulation pipelines for training military AI systems.

title: Autonomous Unmanned Aircraft Systems for Enhanced Search and Rescue of Drowning Swimmers: Image-Based Localization and Mission Simulation
summary: Drowning is an omnipresent risk associated with any activity on or in the water, and rescuing a drowning person is particularly challenging because of the time pressure, making a short response time important. Further complicating water rescue are unsupervised and extensive swimming areas, precise localization of the target, and the transport of rescue personnel. Technical innovations can provide a remedy: We propose an Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS), also known as a drone-in-a-box system, consisting of a fleet of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) allocated to purpose-built hangars near swimming areas. In an emergency, the UAS can be deployed in addition to Standard Rescue Operation (SRO) equipment to locate the distressed person early by performing a fully automated Search and Rescue (S&R) operation and dropping a flotation device. In this paper, we address automatically locating distressed swimmers using the image-based object detection architecture You Only Look Once (YOLO). We present a dataset created for this application and outline the training process. We evaluate the performance of YOLO versions 3, 5, and 8 and architecture sizes (nano, extra-large) using Mean Average Precision (mAP) metrics mAP@.5 and mAP@.5:.95. Furthermore, we present two Discrete-Event Simulation (DES) approaches to simulate response times of SRO and UAS-based water rescue. This enables estimation of time savings relative to SRO when selecting the UAS configuration (type, number, and location of UAVs and hangars). Computational experiments for a test area in the Lusatian Lake District, Germany, show that UAS assistance shortens response time. Even a small UAS with two hangars, each containing one UAV, reduces response time by a factor of five compared to SRO.

AI.tldr: Researchers propose a drone-in-a-box system for water rescue, using YOLO-based object detection to locate drowning persons and discrete-event simulation to optimize drone configurations, showing significant response time reductions.
AI.motivation: Water rescue faces challenges including time pressure, unsupervised swimming areas, difficulty localizing targets, and transporting rescue personnel. Technical innovations like drone systems can address these issues by providing faster response times and automated search capabilities.
AI.method: 1) Developed a dataset for drowning person detection and trained YOLO versions 3, 5, and 8 with different architecture sizes (nano to extra-large), evaluating performance using mAP metrics. 2) Created two Discrete-Event Simulation approaches to model response times for both Standard Rescue Operations and UAS-based systems, enabling optimization of drone configurations (type, number, location).
AI.result: YOLO-based object detection showed promising performance for locating distressed swimmers. Simulation experiments in Germany's Lusatian Lake District demonstrated that UAS assistance significantly shortens response times - even a small system with two hangars (each containing one UAV) reduced response time by a factor of five compared to standard rescue operations.
AI.conclusion: Drone-in-a-box systems with automated object detection using YOLO can dramatically improve water rescue response times. The combination of computer vision for target localization and discrete-event simulation for system optimization provides a comprehensive approach to enhancing drowning rescue operations.

title: Decision-Aware Attention Propagation for Vision Transformer Explainability
summary: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become a dominant architecture in computer vision, yet their prediction process remains difficult to interpret because information is propagated through complex interactions across layers and attention heads. Existing attention based explanation methods provide an intuitive way to trace information flow. However, they rely mainly on raw attention weights, which do not explicitly reflect the final decision and often lead to explanations with limited class discriminability. In contrast, gradient based localization methods are more effective at highlighting class specific evidence, but they do not fully exploit the hierarchical attention propagation mechanism of transformers. To address this limitation, we propose Decision-Aware Attention Propagation (DAP), an attribution method that injects decision-relevant priors into transformer attention propagation. By estimating token importance through gradient based localization and integrating it into layer wise attention rollout, the method captures both the structural flow of attention and the evidence most relevant to the final prediction. Consequently, DAP produces attribution maps that are more class sensitive, compact, and faithful than those generated by conventional attention based methods. Extensive experiments across Vision Transformer variants of different model scales show that DAP consistently outperforms existing baselines in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations, indicating that decision aware propagation is an effective direction for improving ViT interpretability.

AI.tldr: DAP (Decision-Aware Attention Propagation) improves ViT interpretability by combining gradient-based token importance with attention rollout for more class-sensitive and faithful attribution maps.
AI.motivation: Current ViT explanation methods have limitations: attention-based methods rely on raw attention weights that lack class discriminability, while gradient-based methods don't leverage transformer attention propagation mechanisms.
AI.method: DAP integrates gradient-based token importance estimation into layer-wise attention rollout, injecting decision-relevant priors into transformer attention propagation to capture both structural flow and class-specific evidence.
AI.result: DAP consistently outperforms existing baselines across ViT variants of different scales in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations, producing more class-sensitive, compact, and faithful attribution maps.
AI.conclusion: Decision-aware propagation is an effective direction for improving ViT interpretability, successfully combining the strengths of attention-based and gradient-based explanation methods.

title: Test-Time Perturbation Learning with Delayed Feedback for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) achieve remarkable performance in sequential decision-making but remain fragile to subtle environmental shifts, such as small changes in object pose. We attribute this brittleness to trajectory overfitting, where VLAs over-attend to the spurious correlation between actions and entities, then reproduce memorized action patterns. We propose Perturbation learning with Delayed Feedback (PDF), a verifier-free test-time adaptation framework that improves decision performance without fine-tuning the base model. PDF mitigates the spurious correlation through uncertainty-based data augmentation and action voting, while an adaptive scheduler allocates augmentation budgets to balance performance and efficiency. To further improve stability, PDF learns a lightweight perturbation module that retrospectively adjusts action logits guided by delayed feedback, correcting overconfidence issue. Experiments on LIBERO (+7.4\% success rate) and Atari (+10.3 human normalized score) demonstrate consistent gains of PDF in task success over vanilla VLA and VLA with test-time adaptation, establishing a practical path toward reliable test-time adaptation in multimodal decision-making agents. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2026-PDF}{https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2026-PDF}.

AI.tldr: PDF is a test-time adaptation framework for Vision-Language-Action models that improves robustness to environmental shifts without fine-tuning, using uncertainty-based data augmentation, action voting, and delayed feedback correction.
AI.motivation: VLAs perform well in sequential decision-making but are fragile to subtle environmental changes due to trajectory overfitting - they over-attend to spurious correlations between actions and entities, reproducing memorized action patterns.
AI.method: PDF uses uncertainty-based data augmentation and action voting to mitigate spurious correlations, an adaptive scheduler for augmentation budget allocation, and a lightweight perturbation module that retrospectively adjusts action logits using delayed feedback to correct overconfidence.
AI.result: Experiments show consistent gains: +7.4% success rate on LIBERO and +10.3 human normalized score on Atari, outperforming vanilla VLA and VLA with test-time adaptation.
AI.conclusion: PDF establishes a practical path toward reliable test-time adaptation in multimodal decision-making agents without requiring verifiers or fine-tuning base models.

title: Can LLM-Generated Text Empower Surgical Vision-Language Pre-training?
summary: Recent advancements in self-supervised learning have led to powerful surgical vision encoders capable of spatiotemporal understanding.
